BYO/custom GCE images for Compute Engine A3/A2 GPU instances are entirely customer-patched. The boundary between 'Google patches' and 'you patch' shifts by cluster mode (Autopilot vs Standard) and driver-install method, which is a common source of unpatched-driver exposure.
risk: A GPU team pins node versions or uses the NVIDIA GPU Operator and assumes Google patches the driver/OS, leaving kernel/CUDA CVEs unpatched on hosts processing proprietary model weights.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/gpus (GPU driver management) and GKE security bulletins https://docs.cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/security-bulletins

probe: Shielded VM — secure boot / vTPM / integrity monitoring defaults
finding: Shielded VM offers UEFI firmware, Secure Boot, vTPM-backed Measured Boot, and integrity monitoring. On Shielded VM images, Compute Engine enables vTPM and integrity monitoring by default. Many Google public images are Shielded-VM-capable. Integrity monitoring compares boot measurements against a known-good baseline to detect boot-sequence tampering.
gap: Secure Boot — the control that actually blocks unsigned bootloaders/kernels/rootkits — is OFF by default, explicitly because it can break unsigned GPU/third-party drivers. This is the exact footgun for AI/GPU fleets: the workloads most likely to need attestation (nodes loading NVIDIA drivers) are the ones where Secure Boot is most likely left disabled for compatibility. vTPM/integrity-monitoring-on-by-default only applies when a Shielded image is used; nothing forces Shielded VM org-wide unless a team sets the compute.requireShieldedVm org policy constraint, which is not part of the May 2024 secure-by-default baseline.
risk: GPU VMs running proprietary training/inference run with Secure Boot disabled (for driver compatibility), so a bootkit or unsigned malicious kernel module on the host goes undetected by the strongest available boot defense.
evidence: https://cloud.google.com/compute/shielded-vm/docs/shielded-vm — 'Compute Engine enables the virtual Trusted Platform Module (vTPM) and integrity monitoring options by default... Compute Engine does not enable Secure Boot by default because unsigned drivers and other low-level software might not be compatible.'

probe: Confidential Computing — Confidential VM / Confidential GKE (SEV-SNP, TDX) and attestation
finding: Confidential VM encrypts memory in use via AMD SEV / SEV-SNP and Intel TDX, with Confidential GKE node pools available, protecting data-in-use from the hypervisor/host. It provides remote attestation: SEV-SNP launch measurements (including virtual firmware) are hardware-attested by the AMD Secure Processor, while bootloader/kernel/userspace measurements are stored in the Shielded VM vTPM (software-attested), backed by Google's Titan root-of-trust. This is a real sovereignty/IP-protection differentiator for sensitive model weights in memory.
gap: Entirely opt-in and not the default — VMs and GKE nodes run without memory encryption unless Confidential Computing is explicitly selected, and it carries machine-type, region, performance, and feature constraints (e.g., limits on certain GPU/accelerator combinations and live migration), so the highest-value AI/GPU workloads often can't or don't enable it. There is no org-wide default forcing Confidential VM. Attestation is a mix of hardware- and software-rooted measurements, so the userspace trust still relies on the vTPM chain rather than pure hardware attestation.
risk: Proprietary model weights are processed in plaintext host memory on standard (non-confidential) instances because Confidential VM was never opted into, leaving data-in-use exposed to a compromised hypervisor or insider with host access.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/confidential-computing/confidential-vm/docs/confidential-vm-overview and attestation https://docs.cloud.google.com/confidential-computing/confidential-vm/docs/attestation-overview

probe: Cloud KMS / CMEK / Cloud HSM — key custody and default encryption
finding: All data at rest in Google Cloud is encrypted by default with Google-managed keys (no configuration). Customers can layer Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) via Cloud KMS to wrap data-encryption keys, with software keys, FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated Cloud HSM hardware keys, and key import. Keys can have IAM separated from compute, scheduled automatic rotation, and CMEK org policy constraints to require CMEK on supported services.
gap: Default encryption uses Google-held keys — Google retains key custody unless the org explicitly adopts CMEK, and CMEK is opt-in per resource/service rather than default. Requiring CMEK across many projects depends on setting (and centrally managing) org policy constraints; absent that, individual teams default to Google-managed keys with no separation of key admin from data admin. CMEK adds rotation and key-destruction operational risk (a destroyed/disabled key renders data unrecoverable), shifting failure modes onto the org.
risk: Sensitive buckets/disks holding source code and model weights are encrypted only with Google-managed keys (no customer key custody or independent revocation), or a team's CMEK key is mis-scoped/destroyed, causing permanent data loss.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/docs/security/key-management-deep-dive (default encryption) and https://cloud.google.com/security/products/security-key-management (Cloud HSM FIPS 140-2 Level 3)

probe: Cloud External Key Manager (EKM) — hold-your-own-key sovereignty control
finding: Cloud EKM lets the customer hold key material in an external partner KMS/HSM (Fortanix, Futurex, Thales, etc.) entirely outside Google; per Google, externally managed keys 'are never cached or stored within Google Cloud.' Two protection levels exist: EXTERNAL (over the internet) and EXTERNAL_VPC (via VPC). This is a strong sovereignty/HYOK control — Google performs crypto operations only by calling out to the external key, so the customer can revoke Google's ability to decrypt by withholding the key.
gap: EKM introduces a hard external availability dependency: if the external key manager is unreachable, KMS returns FAILED_PRECONDITION and dependent data becomes inaccessible, and some services delete resources after a sustained outage (e.g., Spanner deletes databases after 30 consecutive days of key inaccessibility). It is fully opt-in and operationally heavy, with latency and uptime now gated on the customer's own key infrastructure — so the strongest sovereignty control is also the easiest to turn into a self-inflicted outage or data-loss event. Not all services support EKM.
risk: An org adopts EKM for IP sovereignty over model weights, but an outage of its external key manager (or a 30-day inaccessibility window) causes a production outage or permanent deletion of a backing datastore.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/kms/docs/ekm — 'You control the location and distribution of your externally managed keys. Externally managed keys are never cached or stored within Google Cloud... If you lose keys that you manage outside of Google Cloud, Google can't recover your data.'

probe: Secret Manager injection into Cloud Run / GKE — scoping, rotation, write-back
finding: Secret Manager integrates natively with Cloud Run and GKE. Cloud Run can mount secrets as volume files (always fetches the latest version at read time, so it tracks rotation) or expose them as environment variables (resolved at instance start; Google recommends pinning to a specific version rather than 'latest'). The GKE Secret Manager add-on (CSI) mounts secrets as volumes with CMEK, fine-grained IAM, audit logging, and managed auto-rotation (configurable rotation interval; minimum 120 seconds / 2 minutes, with no default — it must be set explicitly via secret-manager-rotation-interval) in GKE 1.32.2-gke.1059000+.
gap: Env-var injection is a documented footgun: values are frozen at instance startup, so a rotated secret does not propagate to running env-var-based services, and 'latest' is discouraged — yet env vars remain the most ergonomic/common pattern, so rotation silently fails to take effect unless volumes are used. The optional GKE 'sync to Kubernetes Secrets' path writes secret material into etcd-backed Kubernetes Secrets (base64, not encrypted unless etcd encryption/CMEK is configured), widening exposure. Per-secret IAM must be deliberately scoped to each workload's service account; broad project-level secretAccessor grants are an easy over-permission.
risk: A rotated database/API credential isn't picked up because a team used env-var injection with 'latest', leaving stale or unrotated secrets live; or secrets synced into Kubernetes Secrets sit unencrypted in etcd and are readable by anyone with namespace-level Secret read access.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/run/docs/configuring/services/secrets (volume vs env var, latest-version behavior) and GKE add-on https://docs.cloud.google.com/secret-manager/docs/secret-manager-managed-csi-component

probe: Cloud Storage access control & Public Access Prevention defaults (secure-by-default org policies)
finding: Cloud Storage supports Uniform Bucket-Level Access (UBLA — disables legacy per-object ACLs, IAM-only) and Public Access Prevention (PAP — blocks allUsers/allAuthenticatedUsers). For organizations created on or after May 3, 2024, Google enforces a 'secure-by-default' org policy bundle that includes UBLA (storage.uniformBucketLevelAccess), disabling service-account-key creation and upload, and blocking Editor on default service accounts. CMEK, VPC Service Controls perimeters, and signed URLs are all supported for buckets.
gap: Public Access Prevention is NOT in the May-2024 secure-by-default bundle — only UBLA is. So a brand-new org gets ACLs disabled but can still make buckets public via IAM unless someone separately enforces storage.publicAccessPrevention at the org/folder/project level. Buckets default to PAP 'inherited' (governed by org policy) rather than 'enforced', so absent an explicit constraint there is no org-wide guarantee against public exposure. Orgs created before the Feb-May 2024 window get none of these defaults retroactively — a large installed base of older multi-team orgs still runs without UBLA or PAP unless manually applied. The doc focuses on how to enable PAP, never stating it is default-off, which obscures the gap.
risk: In an older org (or a new org that didn't add the PAP constraint), a team grants allUsers on a bucket holding proprietary source/model artifacts and the platform does nothing to stop it, causing public data exposure.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/secure-by-default-organizations (enforced constraints list) and PAP https://docs.cloud.google.com/storage/docs/public-access-prevention

probe: Google Cloud Marketplace — third-party image trust and vetting
finding: Marketplace VM and container products go through a Google review that verifies deploy/uninstall, runs unit tests, and scans the VM image for vulnerabilities; products undergo technical, security, and compliance review, and apps requesting restricted user data are assessed by a third-party firm against OWASP ASVS. Private Marketplace lets an org govern which Marketplace solutions are allowable and identifies container images by SHA / Cloud Run images by metadata annotations.
gap: Vetting is a point-in-time admission check, not continuous assurance: the scan happens at submission, so post-listing image updates, embedded dependencies, and ongoing CVE drift are the publisher's responsibility, and the public docs don't specify scan depth, severity thresholds, or re-review cadence. Marketplace third-party images are not covered by Binary Authorization attestation by default — an org must still wrap them in its own attestation/allowlist policy to gate deployment. Without Private Marketplace configured, any project user can deploy any public Marketplace image, expanding the supply-chain trust surface for a multi-team org.
risk: A team deploys a third-party Marketplace VM/container carrying an outdated or backdoored dependency into a project alongside proprietary IP, trusting a one-time Google review that doesn't cover the image's current state.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/marketplace/docs/partners/vm/submitting (review includes vulnerability scanning of VM image) and Private Marketplace https://docs.cloud.google.com/marketplace/docs/governance/private-marketplace

probe: CMEK dedicated key-project + key IAM as an independent cross-project blast-radius boundary (compromised data project cannot grant itself decrypt)
finding: OPT-IN. Cloud KMS documents a recommended 'centralized' key-management model in which CMEKs live in a dedicated key project (often one per environment folder), separate from the resources they protect. The separation-of-duties guide states 'In the centralized model, your CMEKs and other keys are stored in dedicated key projects, kept separate from the resources they protect' and that the centralized model relies on 'Project boundaries: Keys and the resources they protect are in different projects.' CMEK best practices is blunter: 'Don't create resources encrypted with CMEK in the same project where you manage Cloud KMS keys,' recommending 'a centralized CMEK key project for each environment folder.' IAM enforces the duty split: key admins get roles/cloudkms.admin (manage lifecycle, no use) while key users/service agents get roles/cloudkms.cryptoKeyEncrypterDecrypter (use, no manage); 'Maintain separate identities and permissions for those who administer your encryption keys and those who use them.' Because the key's IAM policy lives in the key project, a principal whose authority is scoped to a data project cannot rewrite that key's IAM binding to grant itself decrypt — the key project is an independent IAM boundary. This is the genuine differentiator: it works precisely because GCP project boundaries are policy/isolation units under the Org->Folder->Project hierarchy. When CMEK access is revoked or the key is disabled/destroyed, 'that data can't be accessed,' so the key project owner holds a cryptographic kill-switch independent of the data project.
gap: The pattern is real and well-documented, but two ergonomic/doc gaps remain for a many-project estate holding proprietary IP. (1) It is entirely OPT-IN and requires deliberate architecture: the default for every CMEK-integrated service is Google-managed keys, and a team that 'just turns on CMEK' can place the keyring in the same data project, collapsing the boundary it was meant to create. The docs warn against this but nothing enforces it by default — there is no default-on org policy that forces keys into a dedicated project (constraints like gcp.restrictNonCmekServices/restrictCmekCryptoKeyProjects exist but are opt-in and must be discovered and configured). (2) The docs describe the model as supporting 'separation of duties' but never explicitly state the breach-containment property the security architect actually cares about — that a compromised data project cannot grant itself decrypt because it has no IAM authority over the separate key project. That cross-project blast-radius reasoning is left to be inferred; the WebFetch of both the separation-of-duties and best-practices pages confirmed neither page discusses compromise scenarios, self-grant prevention, or blast-radius containment. Additionally, an Organization Admin / owner at a parent folder still inherits authority over both the key project and data projects, so the boundary holds only against project-scoped compromise, not against over-broad inherited basic roles higher in the hierarchy.
risk: If a single data project is breached in the multi-team org, a correctly built dedicated-key-project pattern means the attacker still cannot decrypt model weights / source / internal documents protected by keys whose IAM policy lives in a separate project they don't control — strong IP-exposure containment. But because the secure pattern is opt-in, undocumented in its key containment property, and easy to collapse by co-locating keys with data, teams that adopt CMEK for compliance optics without the dedicated-key-project + IAM-split architecture get the audit checkbox with little of the cross-project isolation, and may believe they have blast-radius protection they do not.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/kms/docs/separation-of-duties ('In the centralized model, your CMEKs and other keys are stored in dedicated key projects, kept separate from the resources they protect'; 'Project boundaries: Keys and the resources they protect are in different projects') and https://docs.cloud.google.com/kms/docs/cmek-best-practices ('Don't create resources encrypted with CMEK in the same project where you manage Cloud KMS keys'; 'Maintain separate identities and permissions for those who administer your encryption keys and those who use them'; roles/cloudkms.admin vs roles/cloudkms.cryptoKeyEncrypterDecrypter)

probe: Default (Google-managed) encryption provides zero cross-project key-IAM isolation
finding: DEFAULT-ON, but provides no customer key-IAM boundary at all. Every Google Cloud resource is encrypted at rest by default with Google-owned and Google-managed keys: 'We encrypt all Google customer content at rest, without any action required by you' and 'We own and manage the keys used in default encryption at rest.' The default-encryption documentation's own comparison table states that for Google default encryption the customer 'Can view key metadata: No,' 'Can manage and control keys: Google,' and 'CMEK organization policies: No.' Cloud Storage's standard-encryption docs confirm 'you can't view or manage these keys or review key usage logs' and that default keys 'might be shared among customers.' There is no customer-controllable key, no key ring, and therefore no IAM policy a customer can write, condition, or revoke. Consequently default encryption confers exactly zero cross-project isolation: the same Google-managed key infrastructure protects resources in every project, and a customer cannot use key IAM as a blast-radius boundary between a breached project and proprietary IP in another.
gap: Default encryption is correctly marketed as 'encrypted at rest by default,' and that is true — but the well-architected control a multi-project org needs is customer key custody usable as an independent IAM/blast-radius boundary, which default encryption does not provide and is easy to mistake for. Because it is the default for all CMEK-integrable services, the path of least resistance for every team is to ship with no customer key isolation. There is no default-on org-policy that requires CMEK; gcp.restrictNonCmekServices is opt-in and must be discovered. The doc-quality risk is that 'encrypted at rest by default' (a familiar, reassuring phrase) implies a protective key boundary that simply isn't there cross-project: data in a breached project and data in a sensitive project share the same Google-managed key custody with no customer-enforceable separation.
risk: In the many-project estate, relying on default encryption to protect proprietary IP gives strong protection against physical-media theft and Google-internal-only threats, but ZERO containment when a project's IAM is compromised — there is no customer key whose access the attacker is missing, because there is no customer key at all. Teams that read 'encrypted at rest by default' as 'isolated' will under-invest in the dedicated-key-project + key-IAM pattern that actually limits cross-project IP exposure.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/docs/security/encryption/default-encryption ('We encrypt all Google customer content at rest, without any action required by you'; 'We own and manage the keys used in default encryption at rest'; comparison table: Can view key metadata: No / Can manage and control keys: Google / CMEK organization policies: No) and https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/encryption/default-keys ('you can't view or manage these keys or review key usage logs')

probe: Confidential GPU (in-use protection of model weights in accelerator memory) GA breadth: machine-family, GPU-model, and regional coverage versus confidential CPU VMs
finding: GCP Confidential Computing is GA and broad for CPU-only VMs (AMD SEV globally on N2D/C2D/C3D; Intel TDX on C3 GA across 10+ regions since 2024-09-27), but confidential protection that actually reaches GPU/accelerator memory — where model weights and training tensors live — is far narrower and only recently GA. Per the release notes, Confidential VM with an attached GPU reached GA on 2025-07-31 and is limited to exactly one configuration: the a3-highgpu-1g machine type with a SINGLE NVIDIA H100 80GB GPU, using Intel TDX on the CPU plus NVIDIA Confidential Computing on the GPU. It was in preview as recently as October 2024. The supported-configurations / GPU-creation docs confirm it is single-GPU only (a3-highgpu-1g), does NOT support multi-node clusters, does NOT support reservations, and requires the Spot or flex-start (MIG) provisioning model. It is opt-in/default-off (enabled only at instance creation). Confidential GPU support is restricted to just three zones (europe-west4-c, us-central1-a, us-east5-a), whereas the underlying a3-highgpu-1g machine type is otherwise available in ~17 regions. No confidential support is documented for A2/A100, multi-GPU A3 (a3-highgpu-8g/megagpu), H200, B200, or TPUs.
gap: For a proprietary-IP org training/serving on GPUs, the well-architected expectation — in-use (memory) encryption that covers the accelerators holding model weights — is only met on a single, single-GPU machine type (a3-highgpu-1g, one H100) in three zones, with no on-demand/reservation capacity (Spot or flex-start only), no multi-GPU and no multi-node-cluster support, and no live migration. The org's larger training fleet (multi-GPU/multi-node A3, A100/A2, H200/B200, TPUs) gets confidential protection on the CPU only, leaving model weights in GPU/TPU memory outside the TEE. The headline 'Confidential Computing is GA' (true for CPU VMs) materially overstates coverage for the GPU stack that actually matters here.
risk: Multi-GPU and multi-node training of proprietary models — the org's primary workload — cannot run under a confidential GPU TEE today, so model weights and training data sit unencrypted in accelerator memory on those instances; teams that assume 'Confidential Computing covers our AI workloads' are protecting the CPU but not the GPU. The single-GPU, 3-zone, Spot/flex-start-only footprint also makes confidential GPU impractical at scale (no guaranteed capacity, no live migration → eviction/maintenance interrupts long training runs).
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/confidential-computing/confidential-vm/docs/release-notes — '2025-07-31 ... Support for accelerator-optimized a3-highgpu-1g machine type for securely running AI and ML workloads is now generally available ... 4th Generation Intel Xeon Scalable processor (Sapphire Rapids), Intel TDX, 1 NVIDIA H100 GPU'; https://docs.cloud.google.com/confidential-computing/confidential-vm/docs/supported-configurations (only A3-highgpu-1g row supports GPU, Intel TDX + NVIDIA, no live migration); https://docs.cloud.google.com/confidential-computing/confidential-vm/docs/create-a-confidential-vm-instance-with-gpu (single H100, spot/flex-start/MIG, three supported zones, no multi-node clusters, no reservations); https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/how-confidential-accelerators-can-boost-ai-workload-security ('As of October 2024, Confidential VM with NVIDIA H100 GPU is in preview on the accelerator optimized A3 machine series')

probe: Confidential GPU attestation verification workflow: is in-use GPU protection accompanied by an automatic, default attestation of the accelerator, or a separate customer-operated process distinct from CPU TEE attestation?
finding: Confidential VM attestation is split into two distinct, non-unified workflows. CPU/TEE attestation produces a report signed by a software vTPM or hardware TSM that a verifier must actively retrieve and validate itself (e.g., with go-tpm-tools), or via Google Cloud Attestation — it is not automatic. GPU attestation is a SEPARATE process: it requires Secure Boot (to load only distribution-signed GPU drivers) plus installing NVIDIA's nvtrust library and using the NVIDIA Remote Attestation Service (NRAS) to verify the H100's VBIOS, returning a token signed by NVIDIA (a third party), not by Google. There is no single fused CPU+GPU attestation artifact documented; the customer must stitch together CPU TEE attestation and NVIDIA-signed GPU attestation and decide policy. Attestation is opt-in tooling, not a default-on gate.
gap: Versus a well-architected expectation of a single, provider-attested, default-on verification that proves both CPU and GPU are in a genuine TEE before secrets/weights are released, GCP requires the org to operate two separate verification chains (Google vTPM/TSM for the CPU, NVIDIA NRAS for the GPU) and build its own policy logic to bind them. This is workable but adds a dependency on NVIDIA's external NRAS and meaningful integration effort; nothing enforces that weights are only decrypted after a passing combined attestation unless the org wires that itself (e.g., via Confidential Space).
risk: An org could deploy a 'Confidential' GPU VM yet never actually verify the GPU is in confidential mode — attestation is not enforced by default — so misconfiguration silently degrades to CPU-only or no protection. The reliance on NVIDIA's NRAS for the load-bearing GPU attestation also introduces a third-party availability/trust dependency in the chain that gates release of model weights.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/confidential-computing/confidential-vm/docs/attestation — attestation reports are 'evidence that your VM is running in a TEE,' signed by 'a software-based vTPM, or a dedicated, hardware-based Trusted Security Module (TSM)'; GPU paths require Secure Boot + NVIDIA nvtrust for 'local or remote attestation,' and 'Remote GPU device attestation uses the NVIDIA Remote Attestation Service (NRAS)' returning a token 'signed by NVIDIA'

probe: Named security leadership: Google Cloud CISO seat (current holder) and the post-Venables structure
finding: Phil Venables, Google Cloud's first CISO (2020-2025), stepped down in March 2025 to a part-time Strategic Security Advisor role. Google Cloud did NOT name a single successor 'CISO'. Instead it consolidated security product leadership under Francis deSouza (COO and President, Security Products, Google Cloud) and operates an 'Office of the CISO' (Nick Godfrey, Senior Director; plus advisors), while Google-wide security leadership sits with Heather Adkins (VP, Security Engineering / Office of Cybersecurity Resilience), Sandra Joyce (VP, Google Threat Intelligence), and Royal Hansen (VP, Security; former Alphabet CISO). The Office of the CISO is a customer-facing advisory body, not the internal accountable security exec for the platform.
gap: For a multi-team org evaluating accountability, the public 'who owns Google Cloud security' picture is diffuse: the marketed 'Office of the CISO' is an advisory/sales-aligned function rather than the internal accountable owner, and no single named platform CISO has been publicly confirmed since the March 2025 departure. Well-architected diligence expects a clearly named, accountable security executive.
risk: Harder to map a single accountable security owner for contract/diligence purposes; the public 'Office of the CISO' brand can be mistaken for the internal accountable security org, which it is not.
evidence: https://cloud.google.com/security/ociso and https://www.googlecloudpresscorner.com/Francis-deSouza ; Venables transition: https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/cloud-ciso-perspectives-phil-venables-on-ciso-2-0-and-the-ciso-factory

probe: Threat intelligence and offensive research org: Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG/Mandiant), Project Zero
finding: Google operates a deep, named threat-research bench: Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG) unifies the former Threat Analysis Group (TAG) and Mandiant (acquired 2022) under Sandra Joyce; Project Zero performs 0-day research; GTIG publishes annual zero-day exploitation analysis (75 zero-days tracked in 2024, down from 98 in 2023) and the Threat Horizons cloud-threat reports. This is a genuine first-tier differentiator versus baseline.
gap: Largely a strength. The only nuance for a customer is that GTIG/TAG/Project Zero are Google-wide research orgs whose output (Threat Horizons, advisories) is informational; operational protection of a customer's own projects still depends on the controls the customer enables (Security Command Center tier, Access Transparency, etc.).
risk: Minimal — research depth is real; the consumer must still operationalize the intel into their own GCP project controls.
evidence: https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/cloud-ciso-perspectives-new-threat-horizons-report-highlights-current-cloud-threats ; GTIG 2024 0-day analysis: https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/google-97-zero-days-exploited-in-2024-over-50-percent-in-spyware-attacks/

probe: Vulnerability Reward Program / bug bounty scope, rewards, and safe harbor
finding: Google runs a famously generous, public, multi-track VRP. A dedicated Google Cloud VRP launched 18 Oct 2024 with a top reward of $101,010 and 150+ products in the top tiers, routing researchers directly to Cloud security engineers via bughunters.google.com. Across all programs Google paid >$17M in 2025 (record, 40%+ YoY) to 700+ researchers. Google is a recognized adopter of legal safe-harbor for good-faith research (no legal action for accidental good-faith policy violations).
gap: Genuine strength. Minor: DoS, social engineering, and SEO-type techniques are explicitly out of scope, and reward tiering is product-dependent, so a niche service holding IP may sit in a lower tier with weaker researcher incentive coverage.
risk: Low — the program is mature, public, and well-funded; residual exposure is only for service classes excluded from scope.
evidence: https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/google-cloud-launches-new-vulnerability-rewards-program ; Cloud VRP rules: https://bughunters.google.com/about/rules/google-friends/cloud-vulnerability-reward-program-rules ; 2025 review: https://blog.google/security/vrp-2025-year-in-review/

probe: Customer-facing incident response: Mandiant IR retainer and what Google shares
finding: Google Cloud sells Mandiant Incident Response services and a Mandiant Retainer with pre-negotiated terms, ~2-hour response SLAs, and the ability to repurpose unused hours for proactive readiness/tabletop/pentest work. Mandiant has frontline breach-response pedigree since 2004. This is a contracted, paid service distinct from baseline platform support.
gap: IR is a separately purchased commercial engagement (retainer/SOW), not bundled into standard support tiers. For a multi-team org, fast IR for a compromise touching proprietary IP requires a pre-negotiated Mandiant retainer in place beforehand; absent that, crisis-time legal/procurement delay is the documented failure mode the retainer exists to avoid.
risk: Without a pre-purchased retainer, time-to-responder during an IP-theft incident is gated by emergency procurement; the platform's standard support tiers do not include forensic IR.
evidence: https://cloud.google.com/security/consulting/mandiant-incident-response-services and https://cloud.google.com/security/consulting/mandiant-retainer ; report an incident: https://cloud.google.com/security/report-incident

probe: Compliance scope and per-product/service-in-scope granularity
finding: Google Cloud holds the full standard suite: SOC 1/2/3, ISO 27001/27017/27018, PCI DSS, FedRAMP (High and Moderate), HIPAA, plus DoD IL4/IL5, surfaced via the Compliance offerings hub and self-service Compliance Reports Manager. Crucially, scope is PER-PRODUCT: each certification lists 'products in scope', and not every service is covered by every framework at every level.
gap: The headline 'Google Cloud is SOC 2 / ISO / FedRAMP certified' obscures that an IP-bearing service (e.g., a specific Vertex AI capability, a GKE add-on, a newer Cloud Run feature) may NOT be in scope of a given attestation until separately authorized. The reader must verify the exact service against each framework's in-scope list rather than rely on platform-level certification claims.
risk: A team may assume a service holding proprietary model weights/source is covered by SOC 2/FedRAMP when that specific service is out of scope, creating a compliance/assurance gap that only surfaces under audit.
evidence: https://cloud.google.com/security/compliance/offerings ; Compliance Reports Manager: https://cloud.google.com/security/compliance/compliance-reports-manager

probe: FedRAMP High and AI/IP-service authorization scope (Vertex AI, GKE)
finding: Google has aggressively expanded FedRAMP High to AI/IP-bearing services: 100+ additional commercial services authorized including Vertex AI Search and Generative AI on Vertex AI, Vertex AI Vector Search, Agent Assist, Agentspace, Cloud Build, and Cloud Run; nine US regions for FedRAMP High. Many High-authorized services are exposed through Assured Workloads. This is current and demonstrably trending toward AI coverage.
gap: Authorization is granular and time-staggered: a specific Vertex AI feature, GPU/TPU configuration, or GKE component holding IP may still lack FedRAMP High or be available only inside Assured Workloads (with its own region/feature restrictions). The exact authorized SKU/feature must be checked in the FedRAMP Marketplace, not inferred from 'Vertex AI is FedRAMP High'.
risk: Selecting an AI service for IP on the assumption of blanket FedRAMP High coverage can land on a non-authorized feature variant; remediation may force a feature downgrade or migration into Assured Workloads.
evidence: https://cloud.google.com/security/compliance/fedramp ; Vertex AI FedRAMP High: https://cloud.google.com/blog/topics/public-sector/vertex-ai-search-and-generative-ai-with-gemini-achieve-fedramp-high

probe: Security advisory / changelog feed and CVE handling
finding: Google Cloud publishes structured Security Bulletins with stable IDs (e.g., GCP-2026-034), severity ratings, linked CVE IDs, affected products (GKE, Compute Engine, Cloud Run, Apigee, etc.), and remediation guidance, plus a subscribable XML feed (cloud.google.com/feeds/google-cloud-security-bulletins.xml). GKE additionally emits SecurityBulletinEvent cluster notifications correlated to the customer's actual cluster version/config.
gap: Strength. Minor: bulletins are spread across product-specific pages (general feed, GKE, Compute Engine, GKE-on-AWS/Azure/bare-metal) so a multi-team org must subscribe to multiple feeds to get full coverage; there is no single unified org-wide severity dashboard that maps bulletins to a specific org's deployed services out of the box.
risk: Low — feeds exist and are machine-readable; risk is operational (must aggregate multiple product feeds) rather than a missing capability.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/support/bulletins ; GKE bulletins: https://docs.cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/security-bulletins

probe: Post-incident transparency: status dashboard and public postmortems
finding: Google Cloud Service Health publishes a public status dashboard with color-coded states, a 365-day history view, up to 5 years of disruption records, and post-incident Incident Summary Reports for major incidents that describe contributing factors and prevention steps. Google has a track record of detailed public retrospectives (e.g., the 2019 networking outage RCA).
gap: Postmortem depth and timeliness are at Google's discretion and vary by incident; summaries are written for the affected service, not tailored to a customer's project-level blast radius. There is no contractual commitment to a fixed RCA-publication SLA, so a customer cannot count on a deep public postmortem for every incident touching their workloads.
risk: For incident accountability around IP-bearing workloads, a customer may receive a high-level summary rather than the forensic detail needed for their own internal RCA, especially for smaller-scope incidents.
evidence: https://status.cloud.google.com/summary ; https://cloud.google.com/support/docs/dashboard

probe: Sovereignty levers: Access Transparency / Access Approval (default-on?) and Assured Workloads
finding: Google offers strong sovereignty tooling: Access Transparency (logs of Google-personnel access to customer content, with justifications), Access Approval (explicit customer consent before Google admin access), Assured Workloads control packages (Regional, EU, ITAR/US-only, plus EU Data Boundary with Access Justifications and Signed Access Approval via Cloud EKM), and sovereign-cloud partnerships (T-Systems in Germany, S3NS in France). However, Access Transparency — while Google now positions it as a default security control for every Cloud organization — still requires an explicit enablement action and a qualifying paid support tier (Standard/Enhanced/Premium); Access Approval and Assured Workloads are likewise opt-in and may carry support-tier/cost prerequisites.
gap: The single most material default-posture footgun in this signal: visibility into Google's own access to customer IP is OFF until a customer explicitly turns on Access Transparency (and pays for a qualifying support tier). A multi-team org assuming it has insight into provider access by default does not. Assured Workloads also constrains available regions/services, so enabling sovereignty controls can remove access to some IP-bearing services.
risk: Proprietary IP can be accessed by Google personnel (for support/operations) with NO customer-visible log until Access Transparency is deliberately enabled; sovereignty assurances the customer believes are in force may be unconfigured.
evidence: Access Transparency (not enabled by default, support tier required): https://docs.cloud.google.com/assured-workloads/access-transparency/docs/enable ; Assured Workloads overview: https://docs.cloud.google.com/assured-workloads/docs/overview ; sovereign controls: https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/new-sovereign-controls-for-gcp-via-assured-workloads

probe: Incorporation, jurisdiction, CLOUD Act exposure, and government data requests
finding: The Google Cloud contracting entity is Google LLC (a Delaware-organized company, US law) — for EU customers, Google Cloud EMEA / Google Ireland may contract — a subsidiary of Alphabet Inc. (NASDAQ: GOOGL/GOOG). As a US-jurisdiction provider, Google is subject to the CLOUD Act: US legal process can compel data under Google's possession/custody/control regardless of physical storage location. Google publishes a Transparency Report on government/law-enforcement requests for enterprise/cloud customer data and a whitepaper on how it handles such requests (reviews, narrows, objects where possible).
gap: Not a control gap but a structural fact the reader must price in: US CLOUD Act reach applies to a US-jurisdiction provider regardless of region selection. Region/residency choices and EU Data Boundary reduce but do not eliminate US-jurisdiction exposure; the strongest available mitigation (customer-held keys via Cloud EKM + Signed Access Approval in Assured Workloads EU) is opt-in and feature-restricting.
risk: Proprietary IP stored in any region can in principle be subject to US legal process; data-residency settings alone do not place IP beyond US jurisdiction without the layered EKM/Access-Approval sovereign controls.
evidence: CLOUD Act/jurisdiction & request handling: https://policies.google.com/terms/information-requests ; government requests for cloud customer data whitepaper: https://services.google.com/fh/files/misc/government_requests_for_cloud_customer_data_google.pdf ; Transparency Report: https://transparencyreport.google.com/user-data/enterprise

probe: Financial stability of the operating entity
finding: Alphabet Inc. (parent of Google LLC) is among the most financially robust cloud operators: Q1 2026 revenue $109.9B, net income $62.6B (incl. one-time equity gain), operating cash flow $45.8B, ~$213.8B current assets, with 2026 capex guidance raised to $180-190B reflecting heavy AI/cloud investment; Google Cloud segment revenue grew ~63% YoY. Long-term debt rose to ~$77.5B but remains modest against cash flow.
gap: No going-concern or vendor-viability concern for the platform itself. Residual risk is strategic, not financial: heavy AI capex and ongoing antitrust/regulatory pressure on Alphabet could influence product roadmap and pricing, and Google's documented history of deprecating services means service-longevity (not solvency) is the real durability question for IP-bearing workloads.
risk: Vendor solvency is not a meaningful risk; the durability risk is service deprecation/roadmap change rather than the company's ability to operate the platform.
evidence: Alphabet Q1 2026 results (SEC 8-K): https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/0001652044/000165204426000043/googexhibit991q12026.htm ; earnings release: https://s206.q4cdn.com/479360582/files/doc_financials/2026/q1/2026q1-alphabet-earnings-release.pdf

probe: Assured Workloads as a residency + personnel-isolation substrate: the service-availability tradeoff (which IP/AI-bearing services you lose inside a compliance boundary) and the exact, regime-specific personnel-access guarantees
finding: Assured Workloads applies control packages at the FOLDER level of the Org->Folder->Project hierarchy, enforcing residency, personnel, and encryption controls via organization policy constraints. It is opt-in (you must create an Assured Workloads folder; nothing is on by default). Two findings dominate. (1) The service-availability tradeoff is REGIME-SPECIFIC, not uniform: each control package has its own supported-products table, and 'If a service is not listed for a given control package, that service is unsupported and has not met all regional, regulatory, or sovereign controls required for the control package.' Critically for an IP/AI org, ITAR does NOT list Vertex AI generative models at all - so an ITAR folder forecloses frontier Gemini/Claude inference. By contrast, IL4, IL5, FedRAMP Moderate/High, and the EU Data Boundary packages DO list 'Generative AI on Vertex AI' (aiplatform.googleapis.com) as supported with restrictions/limitations listed as 'None.' Enforcement is real: the gcp.restrictServiceUsage org policy restricts runtime access to supported products only, gcp.resourceLocations pins regions (e.g. IL5 = us, us-central1...us-west4 only), and gcp.restrictNonCmekServices forces CMEK. (2) Personnel-access guarantees are narrower than 'Google personnel cannot access your data' marketing implies. The concrete, package-level guarantee is support-case ROUTING: IL4/IL5 = 'support cases are routed to US Persons located in the US'; EU Data Boundary and Support = 'routed to EU personnel located in the EU'; FedRAMP High requires support personnel/subprocessors located in the US with enhanced background checks. Broader operator-access restriction is delivered by SEPARATE opt-in controls bundled in 'and Support'/Sovereign variants - Access Approval and Key Access Justifications - not by the residency boundary itself, and the EU package's own docs warn it 'doesn't provide data residency controls for in-use data and in-transit data' and that features like Guest environment scripts / VM Manager OS policies can access unencrypted data.
gap: Versus well-architected best practice, the load-bearing detail is buried in per-package supported-products tables rather than surfaced as a single decision matrix, so a residency/IP-sensitive org cannot tell from the top-level 'Assured Workloads = present' framing that (a) choosing ITAR silently removes Vertex AI generative models - the most IP-relevant service - while IL4/IL5/FedRAMP/EU retain them, and (b) the headline 'restricted Google personnel access' resolves, at the package level, to support-case routing by citizenship/location plus opt-in Access Approval / Key Access Justifications, NOT a default blanket bar on Google operator access. The control is also folder-scoped and opt-in: existing projects holding source code or model weights are not protected unless explicitly placed in (or migrated into) an Assured Workloads folder, and migration of an existing project into the boundary is constrained.
risk: A multi-team IP org that reaches for Assured Workloads expecting both data residency AND strong personnel isolation can pick the wrong package and either (i) lose access to the exact AI/IP services it needs (ITAR folder = no Vertex AI generative models, forcing a re-architecture or a fallback to FedRAMP/IL packages), or (ii) over-trust the personnel guarantee, assuming Google operators are categorically locked out of model weights/source when the package alone only guarantees support-case routing - true operator-access denial requires also enabling Access Approval/Key Access Justifications and avoiding features (Guest environment, VM Manager OS policies) that can touch unencrypted data. Either misread is discoverable only by reading multiple per-package tables.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/assured-workloads/docs/supported-products (states unlisted services are unsupported/non-compliant; ITAR table omits Vertex AI generative models). IL5 personnel + policies: https://docs.cloud.google.com/assured-workloads/docs/control-packages/il5 ('support cases are routed to US Persons located in the US'; gcp.resourceLocations US-only; gcp.restrictServiceUsage; gcp.restrictNonCmekServices). IL4: https://docs.cloud.google.com/assured-workloads/docs/control-packages/il4 (Generative AI on Vertex AI supported, restrictions 'None'; support routed to US Persons). EU Data Boundary supports Gen AI on Vertex AI with no restrictions: https://docs.cloud.google.com/assured-workloads/docs/control-packages/eu-data-boundary . EU + Support personnel/Access Approval/KAJ limits: https://docs.cloud.google.com/assured-workloads/docs/control-packages/eu-data-boundary-support . Overview (folder-level constraints, personnel controls, package variation): https://docs.cloud.google.com/assured-workloads/docs/overview

probe: Shared responsibility model: does GCP explicitly state what is NOT its security boundary and that responsibility varies by service type?
finding: The Architecture Framework documents responsibility split honestly and by service tier. For IaaS (Compute Engine, Cloud Storage, Persistent Disk) it states 'the bulk of the security responsibilities are yours'; for PaaS (GKE, BigQuery) you 'share responsibility...for application-level controls and IAM management'; for FaaS/serverless (Cloud Run) the split shifts further to Google for infrastructure but you 'remain responsible for your access controls.' Google also openly augments this with a 'shared fate' framing, explicitly arguing the bare shared-responsibility model 'stops short of helping cloud customers achieve better security outcomes,' and acknowledges that most breaches stem from customer misconfiguration. This is a candid, well-architected statement of the boundary.
gap: The framing is positive (who owns what) rather than a crisp negative statement of 'this is NOT a boundary.' For a multi-team org mixing IaaS GPU VMs, GKE, and Cloud Run under one Organization, the responsibility boundary silently shifts per service, and the doc does not give a per-project enforcement checklist; teams must infer their share from prose. 'Shared fate' deliverables (blueprints, landing zones) are guidance, not enforced defaults.
risk: A team spinning up an A3 GPU instance inherits near-total config responsibility (firewall, IAM, OS patching, disk encryption keys) while assuming the platform's 'secure by default' reputation covers more than it does. The boundary shift between Cloud Run and Compute Engine is easy to miss across many projects.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/architecture/framework/security/shared-responsibility-shared-fate -- 'For IaaS...the bulk of the security responsibilities are yours, and our responsibilities are focused on the underlying infrastructure and physical security.' and 'Google believes that the shared responsibility model stops short of helping cloud customers achieve better security outcomes. Instead...we believe in shared fate.'

probe: Access Transparency: does Google log its OWN staff access to customer content, and is it default-on?
finding: Access Transparency logs the actions Google personnel take when accessing Customer Data, recording justification (e.g. a support ticket reference) and is distinct from Cloud Audit Logs (which record customer actions). Critically, it is enabled by default: 'Access Transparency is a default security control for every Google Cloud organization.' Coverage spans 80+ services including Compute Engine, Cloud Storage, Cloud KMS, IAM, GKE, and Vertex AI. This is a genuine transparency differentiator that few providers match by default.
gap: Default-on is the headline, but the value depends on the org actually ingesting and alerting on these logs (they land in Cloud Logging and must be routed/monitored). Coverage is service-by-service with GA vs Preview tiers, so newer AI services may be Preview or absent. The control logs access but, by itself, does not block it (that requires the opt-in Access Approval).
risk: A multi-team org holding proprietary IP gets visibility into Google-staff access by default, but without a log sink and alerting policy the logs sit unwatched. Reliance on the 'default-on' label can create false comfort if no one is reading the logs.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/assured-workloads/access-transparency/docs/enable -- 'Access Transparency is a default security control for every Google Cloud organization.'; coverage at https://docs.cloud.google.com/assured-workloads/access-transparency/docs/supported-services

probe: Access Transparency exclusions: is Google honest about what staff access is NOT logged?
finding: Google publishes an explicit exclusions page listing five categories of personnel access that are intentionally NOT captured: (1) access Google is 'legally prohibited' from notifying you about; (2) access you authorized via IAM (logged in Cloud Audit Logs instead); (3) access not targeting Customer Data (e.g. aggregate queries across multiple customers); (4) access to public resource identifiers (project IDs, bucket/VM names); and (5) access originating from 'Google's standard automated systems and code' that passed code-authorization and security reviews. This is an honest, enumerated boundary rather than a marketing claim of total coverage.
gap: The 'standard automated systems and code' exclusion is broad and undefined from the customer's vantage point: automated pipelines that touch Customer Data without a human are not surfaced. The 'legally prohibited' exclusion means national-security/legal-process access is invisible. Neither exclusion is quantified, so a customer cannot size how much access falls outside the log.
risk: For an org with sensitive model weights and source code, the 'automated systems' and 'legally prohibited' carve-outs are the exact scenarios of highest concern, and they are precisely what Access Transparency does not show. The transparency tool's honesty is real, but the residual blind spot is structurally invisible.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/assured-workloads/access-transparency/docs/exclusions -- exclusions include access where 'Google is legally prohibited from notifying you' and access that 'originates from Google's standard automated systems and code.'

probe: Access Approval (require approval before Google staff access): default state and break-glass behavior
finding: Access Approval lets a customer require explicit approval before Google personnel access Customer Data, a strong control. But it is opt-in: it (and its dependency Access Transparency) must be enabled, then services individually or wholesale enrolled. Crucially, Google documents an honest carve-out: 'the approval request may be automatically approved without the customer's action to address time-sensitive outages,' logged in an 'auto approved' state (auto-approval is disabled for Sovereign Controls workloads). The docs also warn enabling it increases Customer Care response time by the duration spent awaiting approval.
gap: Two footguns: (1) it is opt-in, so the default posture is 'Google can access without your approval'; and (2) even when enabled, the time-sensitive-outage auto-approval path means the gate is not absolute for general (non-Sovereign-Controls) customers. The auto-approval is logged but not blockable, so 'require my approval' is weaker than the term implies outside Sovereign Controls.
risk: An org that enables Access Approval believing it has a hard gate on Google access to model weights/IP may find an outage triggered an auto-approved access it never sanctioned. Getting a true hard gate requires Sovereign Controls / Assured Workloads tiers (additional cost and Enhanced/Premium support).
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/assured-workloads/access-approval/docs/overview -- 'the approval request may be automatically approved without the customer's action to address time-sensitive outages.' and that the dependency Access Transparency must first be enabled.

probe: Access Transparency coverage for AI/GPU workloads (Vertex AI): are the gaps disclosed?
finding: Vertex AI is in scope for Access Transparency for most surfaces (training, online/batch inference, Feature Store, Model Monitoring, Pipelines), and Google documents the exclusions in a dedicated Limitations section. But the excluded scenarios are exactly the ones a serious AI shop uses: 'Using custom containers to serve online inference or batch inference requests from custom-trained models,' Vertex Explainable AI with custom containers, AutoML forecasting resources, Vertex AI Vector Search 2.0, RAG Engine, and Vizier. So custom-container inference/serving of your own models is a documented blind spot.
gap: An org running proprietary model weights almost always uses custom containers for serving and explainability, which is precisely where Google-staff access is NOT logged. The exclusion is documented but buried in a Limitations subsection, easy to miss when the headline reads 'Access Transparency supports Vertex AI.'
risk: The org's highest-value asset (custom-trained model weights served via custom containers) sits in the one Vertex AI configuration where Google-personnel access lacks transparency logs. The reputation of 'AT covers Vertex AI' overstates protection for the realistic deployment.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/docs/general/access-transparency -- excludes 'Using custom containers to serve online inference or batch inference requests from custom-trained models' and 'Using Vertex Explainable AI with custom containers.'

probe: Basic roles (Owner/Editor/Viewer) over-permissioning: does Google document the footgun honestly?
finding: Google is forthright. The Roles overview states 'In production environments, do not grant basic roles unless there is no alternative,' explains that 'Basic roles include thousands of permissions across all Google Cloud services,' and steers users to predefined or custom least-privilege roles. This is a credit-worthy, explicit warning against the platform's own legacy default rather than glossing over it.
gap: The honesty is in the docs, not the defaults: basic roles remain easy to grant in the console (and the Editor role is still auto-granted to the default service account / common in quickstarts), so the warning is opt-in reading while the footgun is one click away. The warning lives in reference docs, not as an inline console nudge at grant time.
risk: Across many lightweight projects in one Organization, it is trivial for a team to grant Editor/Owner for convenience, accumulating cross-service privilege (delete Firestore, overwrite BigQuery, exfiltrate Cloud Storage) far beyond intent. The doc warns, but nothing default-blocks it.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/roles-overview -- 'In production environments, do not grant basic roles unless there is no alternative. Instead, grant the most limited predefined roles or custom roles that meet your needs.'

probe: Do IAM Conditions ('scoped' access) deliver, or does support vary silently by service?
finding: IAM Conditions exist but their reach is uneven and the failure modes are quiet. The overview admits 'Only some resource types accept conditions in role bindings.' More dangerously, the attribute-reference docs warn that if a condition uses an attribute not available for a given resource, 'that part of the condition is never interpreted as granting access' (silent never-grant), and that Cloud Storage API attributes in a conditional binding cause 'Cloud Storage methods [to] work incorrectly and fail unexpectedly.' Cloud Storage conditions also require uniform bucket-level access to be enabled first. Conditional bindings also never override an unconditional binding of the same role.
gap: 'Scoped' IAM reads as universal but is service-conditional. The silent-never-grant behavior and the Cloud Storage attribute trap mean an engineer can write a least-privilege condition that either does nothing (over-grant via fallback) or breaks the API (mis-scope), with no upfront error. The real per-service support matrix lives on a separate 'resource types that accept conditional role bindings' page that must be consulted manually.
risk: A team trying to write genuine least privilege on Cloud Storage holding proprietary IP may produce a binding that silently fails open or breaks reads, undermining the whole point of conditions. Ergonomically, least privilege is hard to write correctly and the failure is invisible.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/conditions-overview (states only some resource types accept conditions; conditional bindings don't override unconditional ones); https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/conditions-resource-attributes ('API attributes for Cloud Storage are supported only in Credential Access Boundaries...Cloud Storage methods will work incorrectly and fail unexpectedly.')

probe: Marketing vs reality: 'BeyondProd' / 'zero trust' / 'secure by design' -- does the term imply more than the customer gets by default?
finding: BeyondProd is a descriptive whitepaper of how GOOGLE secures its OWN internal microservices ('how Google implements security in our infrastructure', 'protect the microservices that we run in our environment'). It is explicitly not a customer-deployable control set; the 'What's next' section points customers to adopt similar principles via external frameworks like SLSA rather than enabling BeyondProd. Google is honest within the document about this scope and dates it (May 2024, with a status-quo disclaimer), but the brand term circulates as if it were a feature a customer's workloads inherit.
gap: 'BeyondProd / zero trust' as a marketing banner implies the customer's GKE/Cloud Run workloads are automatically protected by service-identity, code-provenance, and mutual-auth controls. In reality the customer must independently assemble the analogues (Binary Authorization, Workload Identity, service mesh mTLS, Cloud Armor) — none of which is default-on. The whitepaper is the honest part; the brand association is what oversells.
risk: Leadership may assume 'we're on Google, so we get BeyondProd zero trust' and under-invest in the customer-side controls (image signing, workload identity, mesh mTLS) that actually deliver it for the org's own services. The platform's internal posture is not the org's posture.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/docs/security/beyondprod -- 'This document describes how Google implements security in our infrastructure using a cloud-native architecture called BeyondProd' and 'BeyondProd helps protect the microservices that we run in our environment.'

probe: API permission-denied error messages: informative without leaking resource existence?
finding: Google strikes a deliberate balance documented in a dedicated page. Errors name the exact missing permission (e.g. 'EMAIL_ADDRESS does not have storage.buckets.list access to the Google Cloud project'), which is excellent ergonomics for writing least privilege. Yet for cases where naming the resource would leak its existence, the message reads 'Permission "storage.buckets.list" denied on resource (or it may not exist)' — intentionally blurring permission-denied vs resource-absent. The console additionally lists the specific permissions needed. This is well-architected: actionable for the authorized, non-leaky toward the unauthorized.
gap: Minor: behavior is inconsistent across services/surfaces — some errors name the permission and resource plainly while others use the ambiguous form, so an engineer cannot rely on a single predictable shape. The 'permissions you need' console hint is not uniformly present in raw API/CLI output.
risk: Low. The main effect is debugging friction (figuring out which of several missing permissions caused a multi-permission operation to fail), not a leakage or over-grant risk. On balance this is a transparency strength.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/permission-error-messages -- example 'EMAIL_ADDRESS does not have storage.buckets.list access...' and the deliberately ambiguous 'Permission ... denied on resource (or it may not exist).'

probe: Public roadmap for security features: can a customer plan against committed future controls?
finding: Google publishes thorough, per-product release notes (Security Command Center, Cloud Armor, IAM, Google Security Operations, plus a unified, BigQuery-queryable, RSS-subscribable release-notes feed) documenting what has shipped. But there is no public forward-looking security roadmap of committed, dated future capabilities. Planning depends on tracking release notes after the fact, the blog, and (for committed dates) NDA/account-team conversations.
gap: For a multi-team org standardizing security controls across many projects, the absence of a public roadmap means it cannot architect against announced-but-unshipped controls (e.g. upcoming IAM Conditions service coverage, new Access Approval-supported services, deprecations) without an account team. Preview-stage features carry no SLA or deprecation guarantee, so building on them is a planning hazard.
risk: Teams either build on Preview features that can change in backward-incompatible ways, or get surprised by deprecations announced only in release notes. Security architecture decisions across the Organization rest on retrospective signals rather than committed forward dates.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/release-notes -- centralized, filterable, BigQuery-exportable release notes for shipped changes; no public forward roadmap is published (Security Command Center notes at https://cloud.google.com/security-command-center/docs/release-notes are likewise retrospective).

probe: Access Approval default state (opt-in, per-service) and break-glass auto-approval: whether 'require Google approval before staff access' is a hard guarantee or a soft default that Google can bypass for outages/incidents
finding: Access Approval is NOT enabled by default — it must be explicitly enabled at the org/folder/project level and services must be enrolled (the enrollment UI offers 'all supported services incl. Preview', 'GA-only', or hand-picked services). Crucially, even when enabled, Google reserves a documented break-glass exception: 'the approval request may be automatically approved without the customer's action to address time-sensitive outages.' The announcement blog states the approval requirement applies 'unless those accesses are required by law or necessary to resolve a current outage or security incident.' So 'require approval before Google accesses your data' is a soft default, not a hard guarantee: during an active outage or security incident (or legal compulsion) Google staff can access Customer Data without the customer's prior approval. The mitigations: such accesses ARE logged — auto-approved requests are recorded in an 'auto approved' state and are still surfaced in Access Transparency logs (with accessor, resource, action, time, and a justification reason code). Auto-approval is NOT time-bounded in the docs (no documented expiry/duration on the bypass). The only documented way to remove the bypass entirely is to deploy under Assured Workloads Sovereign Controls or Sovereign Controls by Partners, for which 'auto-approval is automatically disabled' — i.e. a hard no-bypass guarantee requires a separate (regulated, region-restricted, higher-friction) product tier, not standard Access Approval. A newer 'audit-only' mode further weakens the default-deny posture: it runs Access Approval in an on-demand/reactive model where access is monitored but not blocked, and approval is only required after a finding is revoked.
gap: Versus well-architected best practice for a multi-team org holding proprietary IP, three weaknesses: (1) Opt-in default-off means a new project under the Organization has NO approval gate on Google-staff access unless an admin explicitly enabled and enrolled it org-wide — easy to forget per-project/per-service, and there is no documented default that auto-enrolls new projects in the hierarchy unless an org-level config was pre-set. (2) The outage/incident/legal break-glass is self-asserted by Google, not customer-gated, and carries no documented time-bound or per-event scope limit on the bypass, so 'approval required' is not a hard guarantee for the exact high-stress moments (incidents) when staff access to IP is most consequential. (3) A true no-bypass guarantee is only reachable via Sovereign Controls/Assured Workloads, a regulated higher-tier product, not via plain Access Approval — the standard control implies more guarantee than it delivers.
risk: For the org's proprietary IP (source code, model weights, internal docs), Google personnel can read Customer Data without prior customer approval during any Google-declared outage or security incident, with no documented time limit on that bypass. The control reduces — but does not eliminate — the provider-insider/legal-compulsion exposure, and only the after-the-fact Access Transparency log (which must itself be enabled and actively monitored) provides accountability for those bypassed accesses.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/assured-workloads/access-approval/docs/overview — 'Access Approval is not enabled by default'; enrollment options incl. default 'Automatically enable…for all supported services'; 'the approval request may be automatically approved without the customer's action to address time-sensitive outages' and 'logged in an auto approved state'; 'Auto-approval is automatically disabled for all workloads deployed with Assured Workloads Sovereign Controls or Sovereign Controls by Partners.' Break-glass legal/outage/incident carve-out: https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/introducing-access-approval-and-new-access-transparency-services-gain-more-meaningful-oversight-of-your-cloud-provider ('unless those accesses are required by law or necessary to resolve a current outage or security incident'). Audit-only/reactive mode: https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/introducing-audit-only-mode-for-access-transparency

probe: Access Approval / Access Transparency coverage gaps for Vertex AI and AI/GPU services, and the structural exclusions that bound what 'require approval' can actually cover
finding: Access Approval supports the relevant services at GA — Vertex AI, Compute Engine, GKE, Cloud Storage, and Cloud Run are all listed as GA on the supported-services page. However, coverage is NOT complete in two ways that matter for AI/GPU IP. First, Vertex AI carries a documented limitations footnote: Access Transparency (and therefore Access Approval) does NOT log Google access to data when using custom containers to serve online/batch inference from custom-trained models, when using Vertex Explainable AI with custom containers, or for AutoML forecasting resources; for Vertex AI Search, access to data is not logged for multi-region data stores using 'global' options, and preview-feature/search-analytics data is excluded. Security controls including Access Transparency are not supported for preview Generative AI models. Second, Access Approval inherits Access Transparency's structural exclusions — no log/approval is generated when: Google is legally prohibited from notifying you; access is via IAM you granted (shows in Cloud Audit Logs instead); the access doesn't target Customer Data; access is only to public resource identifiers (project IDs, bucket/VM/cluster names, BigQuery resource IDs); or 'the access originates from Google's standard automated systems and code' validated by code authorization. The automated-systems exclusion is broad and uncapped: a large class of programmatic Google access is categorically outside the approval gate.
gap: For an org running its own models on Vertex AI / GPU compute, the exact surfaces where proprietary model weights and training data live (custom-trained model serving via custom containers, Explainable AI with custom containers) are documented blind spots: Google access there is neither logged by Access Transparency nor gated by Access Approval. The 'standard automated systems / code authorization' exclusion further means a broad category of Google-internal programmatic access never reaches the approval workflow at all — code-review-based authorization substitutes for customer approval, which is a control the customer cannot inspect or veto. So 'Vertex AI is GA-supported for Access Approval' overstates coverage relative to where the sensitive IP actually resides.
risk: Model weights/training data served via custom containers, and any Google access classified as 'automated/code-authorized', can be accessed without an Access Approval prompt and may not appear in Access Transparency logs at all — the very assets (proprietary models) and access patterns most relevant to the IP threat model sit partly outside the control's coverage, and the gap is only findable by chasing a footnote off the supported-services table.
evidence: Supported services (GA rows + Vertex AI limitations footnote): https://docs.cloud.google.com/assured-workloads/access-approval/docs/supported-services. Vertex AI logging limitations (custom containers, Explainable AI, AutoML forecasting): https://docs.cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/docs/general/access-transparency. Access Transparency exclusions inherited by Access Approval (legal, IAM-granted, non-Customer-Data, public identifiers, automated systems/code authorization): https://docs.cloud.google.com/assured-workloads/access-transparency/docs/exclusions. Vertex AI Search global multi-region / preview exclusions: https://docs.cloud.google.com/generative-ai-app-builder/docs/access-transparency

probe: VPC Service Controls: data-exfiltration perimeter (default-on vs opt-in, service coverage for IP-holding services)
finding: VPC Service Controls (VPC-SC) is GCP's marquee anti-exfiltration control: a service perimeter around projects that blocks API access to restricted services from outside the perimeter even when the caller presents valid OAuth/service-account credentials. It is genuinely strong and explicitly designed for the 'stolen credentials / malicious insider' threat. Coverage is broad and GA for exactly the services that hold this org's IP: Cloud Storage, BigQuery (plus Storage/Reservation/Connection APIs), Vertex AI, Artifact Registry, Compute Engine (including restricting persistent-disk snapshots and custom images to the perimeter), and Cloud Run are all supported and protectable. It is included at no additional charge. CRITICAL caveat for this context: it is OPT-IN and default-OFF. A new Google Cloud Organization has no access policy and no perimeter; until an org admin explicitly creates an access policy and a service perimeter, credentials stolen from any project can read Cloud Storage/BigQuery/Vertex AI data from anywhere on the internet.
gap: VPC-SC is the single most important anti-exfil control for a multi-team org holding proprietary IP, yet it is entirely opt-in and is NOT part of GCP's secure-by-default org baseline. There is no automatic perimeter around an Organization's projects; the IP-holding services (GCS, BigQuery, Vertex AI, Artifact Registry) are exfiltratable with valid credentials until an admin manually designs and enforces perimeters. Out of the box the control provides zero protection.
risk: Stolen service-account credentials or a malicious insider can copy model weights, source artifacts in Artifact Registry, or training data from Cloud Storage/BigQuery to an attacker-controlled project/network, and nothing stops it unless a perimeter was proactively built and enforced.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/vpc-service-controls/docs/overview — 'helps to minimize unwarranted data exfiltration risks'; mitigates 'data exfiltration presented by clients using stolen OAuth or service account credentials.' Supported products (all GA): https://docs.cloud.google.com/vpc-service-controls/docs/supported-products . No charge: https://cloud.google.com/vpc-service-controls/pricing

probe: VPC-SC ergonomics: dry-run vs enforced workflow, ingress/egress rules, configuration complexity
finding: VPC-SC supports a dry-run mode that logs (but does not block) requests violating a proposed perimeter, letting teams measure blast radius before enforcing — a genuinely good safety feature; dry-run violations are auditable via the metadata.dryRun=True field in audit logs, and the documented workflow is dry-run, analyze denials, then enforce. Cross-perimeter and cross-project access requires explicit ingress/egress rules and access levels (context-aware: identity type, identity, device, network origin). However, ergonomics are a known pain point: any single request touching resources in two different perimeters fails with RESOURCES_NOT_IN_SAME_SERVICE_PERIMETER, requiring carefully paired egress policy on one side and ingress policy on the other. Many first-party services (Container/Artifact Registry, Vertex AI, Security Command Center) have service-specific perimeter quirks documented in dedicated troubleshooting pages.
gap: The control is powerful but operationally heavy: correct least-privilege configuration across many projects/perimeters demands paired ingress/egress rules and per-service exceptions. Misconfiguration tends to fail-closed (breaks legitimate workflows) which pressures teams toward overly broad egress rules or abandoning enforcement — eroding the anti-exfil value. Whole categories of GCP docs exist solely to debug perimeter breakage.
risk: For a multi-team org with shared services (e.g. a central Artifact Registry, cross-project Vertex AI pipelines), getting perimeters right is hard; teams may grant broad egress exceptions that quietly reopen the exfiltration path the perimeter was meant to close.
evidence: Dry-run: https://docs.cloud.google.com/vpc-service-controls/docs/dry-run-mode . Cross-perimeter error and remediation: https://cloud.google.com/vpc-service-controls/docs/troubleshoot-services-within-perimeter . Service-specific troubleshooting: https://cloud.google.com/vpc-service-controls/docs/troubleshooting

probe: Default network footgun: auto-created 'default' VPC ships with internet-open SSH/RDP firewall rules
finding: Every new project auto-creates a 'default' auto-mode VPC pre-populated with four named firewall rules at priority 65534: default-allow-internal (TCP/UDP all ports + ICMP from 10.128.0.0/9), default-allow-ssh (TCP 22 from 0.0.0.0/0), default-allow-rdp (TCP 3389 from 0.0.0.0/0), and default-allow-icmp (ICMP from 0.0.0.0/0). The implied rules are sane (implied deny ingress, implied allow egress to 0.0.0.0/0), but the named default rules expose SSH and RDP to the entire internet on any VM that uses the default network without a public-IP/firewall change.
gap: This is a classic misconfiguration-by-default footgun. A developer who spins up a VM in the default network and assigns an external IP immediately exposes port 22/3389 to the entire internet. The opt-out (org policy compute.skipDefaultNetworkCreation) is NOT enforced in GCP's secure-by-default baseline, so even Organizations created after May 2024 still get the default network and these rules in every new project.
risk: With many lightweight projects under one Organization, any team can inadvertently stand up an internet-facing SSH/RDP surface on a GPU/Compute Engine VM, giving brute-force / exploit attackers a direct path to instances that may hold or access proprietary IP.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/firewall/docs/firewalls (default network rules table: default-allow-ssh tcp:22 source 0.0.0.0/0, default-allow-rdp tcp:3389 source 0.0.0.0/0). https://docs.cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/vpc (each new project starts with an auto-mode default network with pre-populated IPv4 firewall rules). Best practice warns against this: https://docs.cloud.google.com/compute/docs/connect/ssh-best-practices/network-access

probe: compute.skipDefaultNetworkCreation org policy and the secure-by-default baseline gap
finding: GCP provides the org policy constraint compute.skipDefaultNetworkCreation to stop new projects from getting a default network (and its open SSH/RDP rules). GCP also ships a 'secure-by-default' org baseline auto-enforced for Organizations created on or after May 3, 2024, which DOES enable seven strong managed constraints (disable SA key creation, disable SA key upload, no automatic Editor on default SAs, domain-restricted IAM, uniform bucket-level access, allowed-contact-domains, and restrict protocol forwarding). Crucially, compute.skipDefaultNetworkCreation is NOT in that baseline. So the default-network footgun is opt-in to fix even on brand-new orgs.
gap: The capability to suppress the dangerous default network exists, but it is left out of the default baseline. Existing/older Organizations get NO baseline constraints at all (the baseline is not retroactive). For a multi-team org, an admin must proactively apply skipDefaultNetworkCreation at the org/folder level; otherwise every new project re-introduces the internet-open SSH/RDP surface.
risk: Orgs that predate May 2024, or that never added the network constraint, silently re-create the default-network exposure each time a team creates a project — the guardrail is available but not on by default.
evidence: Secure-by-default baseline (7 constraints, enforced for orgs created on/after May 3, 2024, not retroactive to existing orgs): https://docs.cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/secure-by-default-organizations . skipDefaultNetworkCreation constraint: https://docs.cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-policy/org-policy-constraints

probe: Hierarchical firewall policies (org/folder) and network firewall policies — centralized guardrails
finding: GCP lets you enforce firewall rules above the project level: hierarchical firewall policies attach to the Organization or to Folders and apply to all VMs in descendant projects, with the property that lower-level rules cannot override a higher-level rule (true central guardrail).
