Global and regional network firewall policies operate at the project/VPC level. Rules can target by network or service account, support goto_next delegation, and Firewall Insights now analyzes hierarchical, global, and VPC-level rules together for overlap/shadowing recommendations.
gap: This is a real strength that maps well to the Org->Folder->Project hierarchy: a central security team can mandate (for example) deny-all-internet-ingress at the org level that no project can override. The gap is purely that it is opt-in configuration — there is no default hierarchical policy, so the central guardrail exists only if a team builds it.
risk: Without an admin authoring org/folder firewall policies, each project is governed only by its own VPC rules (including the open default-network rules), so a misconfigured team project is not contained by any org-wide network guardrail.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/firewall/docs/firewall-policies (hierarchical policies attach to org/folders; lower-level rules cannot override higher-level rules; target by network/SA). Firewall Insights for policies: https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/announcing-firewall-insights-support-for-firewall-policies

probe: Egress control and VPC Flow Logs default state
finding: GCP's implied egress rule allows all outbound traffic to 0.0.0.0/0 by default, so workloads can reach arbitrary internet endpoints unless egress is explicitly restricted. Egress can be tightened via egress firewall rules, VPC-SC egress rules, and Secure Web Proxy (SWP), whose policy notably defaults to deny-all outbound (good posture, but SWP is a separate opt-in product). Cloud NAT gives private (no-external-IP) VMs outbound internet access. VPC Flow Logs — essential for detecting exfiltration over the network — are DISABLED by default and must be enabled per subnet (or mandated org-wide via compute.requireVpcFlowLogs).
gap: Two default-off weaknesses compound the exfil risk: (1) default-allow egress means data can leave to any internet endpoint unless an egress rule, VPC-SC egress restriction, or SWP is configured; (2) VPC Flow Logs are off by default, so absent explicit enablement there is no network-flow record to detect or investigate exfiltration. SWP's good default-deny posture only helps if the org adopts SWP, which is non-trivial.
risk: A compromised workload or insider can stream model weights or source out over standard egress, and with Flow Logs off the org has no network telemetry to detect the transfer or scope the incident.
evidence: Implied allow egress to 0.0.0.0/0: https://docs.cloud.google.com/firewall/docs/firewalls . Cloud NAT: https://docs.cloud.google.com/nat/docs/overview . Secure Web Proxy default-deny: https://docs.cloud.google.com/secure-web-proxy/docs/overview . Flow Logs off by default / org enforcement: https://docs.cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/org-policy-flow-logs and https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/using-flow-logs

probe: Private connectivity: Private Google Access, Private Service Connect, Shared VPC
finding: GCP offers a full private-connectivity stack so workloads need not have public IPs or traverse the internet: Private Google Access lets internal-only VMs reach Google APIs/services privately; Private Service Connect (the PrivateLink equivalent) exposes a producer service as a single consumer-side IP with unidirectional, service-scoped access (not full VPC peering); Cloud Interconnect/Cloud VPN connect to corp; and Shared VPC lets a host project own the network while many service projects attach — a clean fit for centralized multi-team networking. PSC composes with VPC-SC to keep API access on private paths.
gap: Capability is strong and complete; all of it is opt-in design work. PSC, Private Google Access, and Shared VPC must be deliberately architected (host project, subnet sharing, endpoint creation). Default project networking does not use private access, so workloads reach Google APIs over public endpoints unless configured otherwise.
risk: Low if architected; the risk is that teams default to public-endpoint access and external IPs because the private path requires upfront network design, widening the attack/exfil surface unnecessarily.
evidence: Private access options: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/private-access-options . Private Service Connect: https://docs.cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/private-service-connect . Private Google Access: https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/private-google-access . Shared VPC host/service model: https://docs.cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/configure-private-services-access

probe: Tenant/project isolation and the VPC global model
finding: Projects are isolated by default: a VPC and its resources belong to one project, and cross-project networking is explicit (Shared VPC or VPC peering) — there is no implicit cross-project network reachability. The VPC itself is a GLOBAL resource (routes and firewall rules are global; only subnets are regional), which differs from AWS's regional VPC model and means a single VPC/firewall posture spans all regions. Combined with the Org->Folder->Project hierarchy, this gives clear isolation units with explicit, auditable cross-project links.
gap: Isolation defaults are sound. The double-edge of the global VPC model: a single firewall rule or default-network rule applies network-wide across all regions, so one over-broad rule (e.g. the default-allow-ssh 0.0.0.0/0) has global reach rather than being contained to a region. Cross-project sharing is explicit, which is good, but Shared VPC concentrates network control in the host project — a high-value target requiring tight IAM.
risk: A misconfigured global firewall rule or an over-permissioned Shared VPC host project can broaden exposure across regions/teams at once; the global model means blast radius of a bad rule is org-wide, not regional.
evidence: VPC is global, subnets regional; cross-project networking via Shared VPC/peering: https://docs.cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/vpc

probe: Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) TCP forwarding for bastion-less zero-trust access
finding: IAP TCP forwarding provides identity-gated, bastion-less access to SSH/RDP and arbitrary TCP on VMs that have NO public IP: traffic tunnels through Google's front end after IAM authorization (roles/iap.tunnelResourceAccessor / iap.tunnelInstances.accessViaIAP), so access is governed by identity rather than network position. It requires only an ingress firewall rule allowing IAP's fixed range 35.235.240.0/20 (and IPv6 2600:2d00:1:7::/64) to the relevant ports — directly replacing the internet-open SSH/RDP exposure of the default network. This is a strong zero-trust pattern for administering GPU/Compute Engine fleets without public surface.
gap: Genuinely good control, but opt-in and easy to leave unused: teams must remove/avoid the default-allow-ssh rule, add the IAP-range rule, and grant the IAP IAM roles. If they instead keep the default open-SSH rule alongside (or instead of) IAP, the internet-facing path remains. IAP's value is realized only when public SSH/RDP is actually removed.
risk: If IAP is offered but the default-network open SSH/RDP rules are not removed, admins may use IAP while attackers still reach port 22/3389 directly — the secure path coexists with the insecure default rather than replacing it.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iap/docs/using-tcp-forwarding (encrypted tunnel for SSH/RDP/TCP to VMs without external IPs; firewall source range 35.235.240.0/20; IAM roles iap.tunnelResourceAccessor / permission iap.tunnelInstances.accessViaIAP)

probe: VPC Service Controls per-service coverage for IP-bearing services (Cloud Storage, BigQuery, Vertex AI + sub-APIs, Artifact Registry) and the Vertex AI generative/online-prediction caveats that silently weaken the perimeter
finding: The core IP-bearing services ARE first-class GA-supported by VPC-SC: Cloud Storage, BigQuery, Vertex AI (aiplatform.googleapis.com), and Artifact Registry all appear on the official supported-products list. The Vertex AI API is a single VPC-SC-protected service that covers endpoints, online prediction, model registry, and Vector Search under one perimeter boundary. However, the per-service reality has documented holes that the generic 'present' classification hides. From the Vertex AI VPC-SC limitations page: (1) 'Request and response logging isn't available with VPC Service Controls' — enabling the perimeter silently disables the very Gemini/generative audit trail an IP-sensitive org would want; (2) 'Model Garden 1-click public (dedicated endpoint) deployments aren't supported within a VPC-SC environment'; (3) online-prediction endpoints and Agent Engine agents MUST be created AFTER the project is added to the perimeter — building them first and adding the perimeter later silently leaves them with public internet access and causes deployment failures (a sequencing footgun, not a hard block). On the generative-AI side, the 'Security controls' matrix marks VPC-SC as supported for GA Gemini models (online/batch prediction, tuning, context caching), embeddings, Imagen, Veo, and RAG Engine — but states plainly 'Security controls are not supported for preview models', and RAG Engine supports VPC-SC/CMEK while data residency and Access Transparency are NOT supported. Vector Search index query, custom training, and private online-prediction endpoints additionally require Private Service Access / restricted-VIP networking that the perimeter alone does not provide. Net: the perimeter is real and covers the named services, but coverage is per-feature, sequencing-sensitive, and excludes preview models and request/response logging.
gap: For a multi-team org whose crown-jewel asset is model weights and proprietary data flowing through Vertex AI, three documented gaps matter: (a) preview/experimental Gemini models fall entirely outside VPC-SC, so any team that pins a preview model for capability reasons is exfiltration-exposed by default and gets no perimeter error to warn them; (b) turning on the perimeter disables request/response logging, forcing a choice between exfiltration protection and generative-AI observability — exactly the trade a well-architected setup should not have to make; (c) the create-endpoint-before-perimeter ordering produces silently public endpoints rather than a deny, which is a misconfiguration-by-sequencing footgun that scales badly across many projects and teams that each spin up their own endpoints. None of these is a missing feature; all are coverage edges that the generic 'VPC-SC supported' label obscures.
risk: A team using a preview Gemini model, or one that created an online-prediction/Agent-Engine endpoint before the project joined the perimeter, can serve or receive proprietary data over a public path that the org believes is sealed — and request/response logging being off under VPC-SC means the exfiltration of prompts/responses (which can carry source code and internal documents) may not even be recorded.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/docs/general/vpc-service-controls#limitations (Vertex AI VPC-SC limitations: 'Request and response logging isn't available with VPC Service Controls'; 'Model Garden 1-click public (dedicated endpoint) deployments aren't supported'; endpoints/Agent Engine must be created after perimeter inclusion). Supported-products list: https://docs.cloud.google.com/vpc-service-controls/docs/supported-products (Cloud Storage, BigQuery, Vertex AI, Artifact Registry all GA). Generative-AI security-controls matrix: https://docs.cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/generative-ai/docs/security-controls ('Security controls are not supported for preview models').

probe: VPC-SC bypass and break-glass vectors around the perimeter: Cloud Build default pools, Google-managed service agents requiring ingress rules, and the restricted.googleapis.com routing/DNS prerequisite without which the perimeter is not actually enforced on egress
finding: VPC-SC's perimeter has several documented seams that an attacker or a careless team can route IP through. (1) Cloud Build: 'VPC Service Controls protection is available only for builds run in private pools; you cannot use VPC Service Controls with builds run in default pools.' Default pools are the out-of-the-box experience, so a build pulling source from a protected repo and running in a default pool is OUTSIDE the perimeter — a documented egress path for source code unless every project is forced onto private pools. Cloud Build's build phase is also not protected, so Cloud Functions/Cloud Run deployments require an explicit ingress rule for the Cloud Build service account to even reach the perimeter, and Pub/Sub-triggered builds are unsupported under VPC-SC. (2) Service agents / restricted Artifact Registry: certain Google-managed repos such as gcr.io/cloud-builders and gcr.io/gke-release 'remain available to all projects regardless of service perimeters', i.e. they are intentionally exempt from the boundary. (3) Routing prerequisite: VPC-SC only blocks API calls; it does NOT by itself force traffic onto Google's private backbone. To actually contain egress, VMs must resolve *.googleapis.com to the restricted.googleapis.com VIP (199.36.153.4/30) via private DNS zones and route there — otherwise traffic can still reach the public *.googleapis.com VIP. Misconfigured DNS/routing means the perimeter is believed-on but not enforcing on the network path, and this setup is a separate, easy-to-miss step rather than a default.
gap: A well-architected perimeter for proprietary IP should fail closed and have no default-on bypass. Here the defaults cut the other way: Cloud Build's default pool is unprotected (the secure path is opt-in private pools), some Google-managed Artifact Registry paths are perimeter-exempt by design, and the perimeter's network enforcement depends on a manually-configured restricted-VIP DNS/route that, if skipped, leaves egress on the public API path while operators believe the boundary is sealed. Across many teams and projects under one Organization, any single project that runs a default-pool build, or whose VPCs lack the restricted.googleapis.com DNS override, becomes a documented exfiltration channel for source code or model artifacts — and Org Policy does not force the private-pool / restricted-VIP posture by default, so guardrails must be authored and enforced per project.
risk: Source code or model weights can leave the org via a Cloud Build default-pool build, a perimeter-exempt managed registry path, or a VPC whose DNS still resolves googleapis.com to the public VIP — all while the VPC-SC perimeter shows as configured, giving false assurance that egress is contained.
evidence: Cloud Build VPC-SC limitations: https://docs.cloud.google.com/build/docs/private-pools/using-vpc-service-controls ('protection is available only for builds run in private pools; you cannot use VPC Service Controls with builds run in default pools'; Pub/Sub triggers unsupported; build logs not stored in default bucket). Artifact Registry/managed repos exemption and Cloud Functions Cloud Build ingress note: https://docs.cloud.google.com/vpc-service-controls/docs/supported-products . restricted.googleapis.com routing requirement: https://docs.cloud.google.com/vpc-service-controls/docs/set-up-private-connectivity and VIP range 199.36.153.4/30 at https://docs.cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/configure-private-google-access .

probe: Cloud Run / Cloud Functions invocation auth defaults (private-by-default, IAM run.invoker)
finding: Cloud Run services are deployed privately by default and reject requests lacking credentials: by default only project owners/editors and principals holding the Cloud Run Admin or Invoker roles can invoke them. Cloud Run enforces this per-service Invoker IAM check by default; the separate organization-policy managed constraint constraints/run.managed.requireInvokerIam (which prevents the invoker check from being disabled) is opt-in and is NOT enforced by default. Cloud Functions v2 (gen2, which runs on Cloud Run) also requires authentication by default and requires explicit opt-in for unauthenticated. This is a genuine, well-architected default-deny posture for the data plane, reusing the same IAM identity system as the control plane.
gap: The default-deny is real but per-service: each new service/function is its own IAM resource, and there is no project- or folder-wide default-deny invoker guardrail unless an org policy is added. In a multi-project org, default-deny per resource still leaves it to each team to avoid the public toggle.
risk: Low intrinsic risk from the default itself; the residual risk is that a single deploy command flag flips a private IP-protected service to fully public (see allUsers probe). The good default can be silently overridden per service.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/run/docs/authenticating/overview — 'deployed privately by default, which means that they can't be accessed without providing authentication credentials in the request'; https://docs.cloud.google.com/functions/docs/securing/managing-access-iam — 'By default, entities that need to invoke an HTTP function must explicitly present authentication credentials'

probe: 'Allow unauthenticated invocations' / allUsers run.invoker public-by-toggle footgun
finding: Public exposure is one flag/binding away: `--allow-unauthenticated` at deploy time, or binding the special principal allUsers to roles/run.invoker, makes a service reachable by anyone on the internet. The console deploy UI presents 'Allow unauthenticated invocations' as a prominent radio button. The toggle requires only run.services.setIamPolicy (held by Owner and Cloud Run Admin), so any team admin can expose a service. There is no separate confirmation that the service holds proprietary IP.
gap: Compared to a hardened baseline, there is no default org-level prohibition on allUsers. Domain Restricted Sharing (which blocks allUsers) is opt-in, not default-on. A workload serving model weights/inference can be made world-reachable by a routine deploy flag with no additional approval gate.
risk: An engineer copying a sample 'gcloud run deploy --allow-unauthenticated' command, or clicking the default-looking radio button, exposes an internal AI/inference endpoint to the public internet, enabling data exfiltration of proprietary IP and uncapped GPU cost abuse.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/run/docs/authenticating/public — 'Assign the Cloud Run Invoker IAM role to the allUsers member type'; '--allow-unauthenticated' flag 'assigns the Cloud Run Invoker IAM role to allUsers'

probe: Domain Restricted Sharing org policy (iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains) to prevent allUsers exposure
finding: The iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains org policy (Domain Restricted Sharing, DRS) blocks binding allUsers/allAuthenticatedUsers in any IAM policy, including Cloud Run invoker bindings — an attempt errors with 'IAM policy update failed'. This is an effective org-wide guardrail against accidental public exposure and is settable at the Organization/folder level, fitting the hierarchy isolation model. Google has since added constraints/run.managed.requireInvokerIam so public services can still be created intentionally under DRS by disabling the per-service invoker check rather than weakening DRS.
gap: DRS is opt-in and OFF by default. A brand-new Organization does not enforce it, so the protective guardrail only exists if an org admin proactively sets it. The capability is excellent but the default is permissive.
risk: Without an org admin proactively enabling DRS at org creation, every project inherits the permissive default and any team can bind allUsers — the strong control is dormant until someone turns it on.
evidence: https://cloud.google.com/blog/topics/developers-practitioners/how-create-public-cloud-run-services-when-domain-restricted-sharing-enforced — 'you can't allow unauthenticated requests to a Cloud Run service if you've also enabled Domain Restricted Sharing ... results in an error: IAM policy update failed'; 'DRS policies also affect the allUsers identities. It can not be used in any IAM policy if the DRS policy is enabled.'

probe: Cloud Run default ingress 'all' — run.app URL bypasses load balancer / Cloud Armor / WAF
finding: The default ingress setting is 'all': 'The default ingress paths and ingress setting allow any resource on the internet to reach your Cloud Run service' directly at the auto-assigned run.app URL, bypassing any external load balancer. Restricting to 'internal-and-cloud-load-balancing' (so the run.app URL is no longer publicly reachable and traffic must come through the LB) is opt-in, enforceable org-wide via the run.allowedIngress org policy. Critically, Cloud Armor attaches only to LB backend services — it does NOT protect the default run.app URL.
gap: The default exposes a public run.app URL that sits in front of (and parallel to) any WAF/LB an operator later configures. Putting a service behind Cloud Armor does NOT close the run.app URL unless ingress is also tightened — a two-step requirement that is easy to half-complete, leaving an unprotected bypass path to the same backend.
risk: A team fronts an inference service with a Cloud Load Balancer + Cloud Armor (WAF + rate limits) but forgets to set ingress to internal-and-cloud-load-balancing; attackers hit the still-public run.app URL directly, bypassing all WAF rules, rate limiting, and DDoS L7 mitigation, abusing GPU compute and probing the app unthrottled.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/run/docs/securing/ingress — 'The default ingress paths and ingress setting allow any resource on the internet to reach your Cloud Run service'; ingress 'all' 'Allows all requests, including requests directly from the internet to the run.app URL'; org policy 'run.allowedIngress'

probe: Edge auth token properties (Google-signed OIDC ID token, audience-scoped, short-lived)
finding: Service-to-service and end-to-edge auth uses Google-signed OpenID Connect ID tokens (JWTs) carrying an audience claim bound to the target service URL (or a custom audience), 'which limits the risk of a replay attack where an intercepted token intended for use with one service is replayed against a different service.' Tokens are fetched from the metadata server, are short-lived, and are exchanged per-audience rather than being a static shared secret. Multiple distinct invoker principals can be granted per service, enabling scoped, least-privilege caller identities. This is a strong, modern edge-auth design (no long-lived API keys required for the IAM path).
gap: The ID token authenticates the caller identity but conveys no fine-grained scoping beyond run.invoker on that service (it is identity, not capability/scope-limited like an OAuth scope or a capability token). Header handling has a footgun: both Authorization and X-Serverless-Authorization are accepted, and if both are present only X-Serverless-Authorization is checked — proxies that forward an end-user Authorization header to a downstream Cloud Run call must understand this precedence.
risk: An edge proxy that passes through a client's Authorization header alongside its own service token can produce surprising auth-precedence behavior; and the binary invoker grant means any authorized caller can invoke all routes of a service, so route-level least privilege must be implemented in app code, not at the edge.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/run/docs/authenticating/service-to-service — 'add a Google-signed OpenID Connect ID token'; 'An audience field is encoded in the token ... limits the risk of a replay attack where an intercepted token intended for use with one service is replayed against a different service'

probe: Cloud Armor / Google edge DDoS: default-on L3/L4 vs opt-in L7 WAF and preconfigured OWASP rules
finding: Always-on, no-opt-in L3/L4 volumetric and protocol DDoS protection is provided to all customers at the Google global load-balancer edge with 'no latency impact.' However, L7/application-layer protection (HTTP floods, OWASP-class attacks) requires 'a user-configured security policy with proactive rules in place.' The OWASP ModSecurity CRS preconfigured WAF rules are NOT enabled by default — they must be explicitly added to a security policy. Adaptive Protection (ML L7 DDoS detection), Advanced Network DDoS, and Threat Intelligence require the paid Cloud Armor Enterprise / Managed Protection Plus tier.
gap: Out of the box a Cloud Run / LB endpoint has no WAF and no L7 DDoS mitigation; both are opt-in, and the most capable defenses (Adaptive Protection ML, advanced network DDoS) sit behind a paid Enterprise SKU. There is no default-on application-layer WAF for new internet-facing services.
risk: An internet-facing inference/API endpoint launched without an explicit Cloud Armor security policy has zero L7 abuse protection — vulnerable to HTTP-flood cost-abuse of GPU compute, injection probing, and scraping of proprietary model outputs — until a team manually builds and attaches a WAF policy and ideally pays for Enterprise.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/armor/docs/cloud-armor-overview — 'always-on protection from L3 and L4 volumetric ... DDoS attacks'; L7 protection 'requires a user-configured security policy with proactive rules in place'; https://docs.cloud.google.com/armor/docs/waf-rules — preconfigured rules based on 'OWASP Core Rule Set 4.22' must be enabled in a policy

probe: Edge rate limiting / inference cost-abuse prevention (Cloud Armor throttle/ban, Apigee Quota/SpikeArrest, LLMTokenQuota)
finding: Cloud Armor offers per-client rate-based rules: 'throttle' (cap requests/connections per client to a threshold) and 'rate_based_ban' (temporarily ban clients exceeding a threshold). Apigee adds SpikeArrest (smooths traffic bursts per second/minute), Quota (contractual per-app limits per hour/day/week/month), and AI-specific PromptTokenLimit / LLMTokenQuota policies that cap consumption by token count — directly relevant to GPU/LLM cost abuse. These are capable, granular abuse-prevention primitives.
gap: All of this is opt-in configuration, not default. Cloud Armor rate limiting requires building a security policy AND the run.app ingress bypass to be closed (else it is sidestepped). Token-aware quotas (LLMTokenQuota/PromptTokenLimit) require Apigee, a separately licensed, heavyweight product — not available to a plain Cloud Run + LB stack. No default request-rate cap exists on a new endpoint.
risk: Without deliberate rate-limit configuration (and Apigee for token-level caps), a leaked or public inference endpoint can be driven to unbounded GPU/TPU spend; the controls exist but every team must assemble them, and the strongest (token quotas) require adopting Apigee.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/armor/docs/rate-limiting-overview — 'throttle' and 'rate_based_ban' actions; https://docs.cloud.google.com/apigee/docs/api-platform/reference/policies/llm-token-quota-policy — LLMTokenQuota policy for token-based limits

probe: API Gateway / Cloud Endpoints OpenAPI request validation NOT enforced
finding: API Gateway and Cloud Endpoints (ESP/ESPv2) accept OpenAPI documents containing required-parameter and type definitions but do NOT enforce them — requests are forwarded to the backend regardless. Per docs: 'Endpoints accepts OpenAPI documents that include required parameter and type definitions, but ESP and ESPv2 don't enforce these definitions and forwards incoming requests to your API.' API Gateway 'supports only primitive types in request parameters' and rejects external $ref. Request body schema validation is effectively not performed at the gateway.
gap: Operators reasonably assume an OpenAPI-driven gateway validates payloads against the schema (as AWS API Gateway request validation and Apigee OASValidation do). It does not. The OpenAPI spec at the edge documents intent but provides no input-validation defense — a Documented-but-Misleading gap where a familiar term (OpenAPI gateway) implies more than it delivers.
risk: Teams relying on API Gateway 'OpenAPI validation' to reject malformed/oversized/injection payloads before they reach an inference or data backend get no such protection; unvalidated input reaches the proprietary service, shifting all validation burden silently to backend code where it is often missing.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/endpoints/docs/openapi/openapi-limitations — 'Endpoints accepts OpenAPI documents that include required parameter and type definitions, but ESP and ESPv2 don't enforce these definitions and forwards incoming requests to your API'

probe: Custom authorizers at the edge (API Gateway JWT/API-key, Apigee policies, IAP, ESPv2)
finding: Multiple custom-authorizer options exist: API Gateway validates signed JWTs against a configured jwks_uri/issuer/audience and supports API keys; ESPv2/Cloud Endpoints performs the same JWT/issuer checks and caches keys 5 min and validated JWTs up to 5 min/expiry; Apigee provides VerifyAPIKey and OAuthv2 VerifyAccessToken policies plus rich policy chaining; and Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) enforces identity + IAM Conditions + context-aware access (device, IP, location) in front of Cloud Run, App Engine, GKE, and Compute Engine. IAP-on-Cloud-Run now protects all ingress paths 'including default run.app URLs and load balancers' in one step, closing the historical run.app bypass for IAP deployments.
gap: Capabilities are strong but fragmented across four products with different config models (OpenAPI extensions, Apigee XML policies, IAP IAM, ESPv2 sidecar) — there is no single first-class edge-authorizer abstraction. IAP context-aware access levels and Apigee are heavier/licensed; ESPv2 requires running a proxy sidecar. None are default; an unfronted Cloud Run service has only the binary run.invoker identity check.
risk: Achieving fine-grained, context-aware edge authorization for proprietary-IP services requires choosing and operating one of several disparate products; teams that skip this get only coarse IAM identity checks, and inconsistent choices across projects fragment the org's edge-auth posture.
evidence: https://cloud.google.com/api-gateway/docs/authenticating-users-jwt — JWT validation via securityDefinitions and jwks_uri; https://docs.cloud.google.com/run/docs/securing/identity-aware-proxy-cloud-run — IAP secures 'all ingress paths to Cloud Run, including the auto-assigned URL and any configured load balancer URL'

probe: Edge observability — Cloud Load Balancing & Cloud Armor request logging default-off and sampling
finding: Load balancer logging (and therefore Cloud Armor request logging, which rides on LB logs) is DISABLED by default for new backend services: 'Logging for new backend services is disabled by default' and the global/classic external Application Load Balancers 'do not enable logs by default.' Even when enabled, log volume is governed by a configurable sampling rate (0.0–1.0); reducing it down-samples Cloud Armor request logs proportionally. Google recommends sampling rate 1.0 while tuning. Apigee analytics, by contrast, is collected as part of the platform.
gap: The edge security telemetry needed to detect/investigate abuse (which requests hit, which WAF rules fired, which clients were throttled/banned) is off by default and, once on, may be partially sampled. There is no default-on edge audit trail for internet-facing traffic; an org must explicitly enable LB logging per backend and set sampling to 1.0.
risk: An internet-facing inference endpoint can be attacked or scraped with no record if LB/Cloud Armor logging was never enabled, or with incomplete records if sampling is reduced for cost — crippling detection and post-incident forensics for proprietary-IP exposure.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/armor/docs/request-logging — 'Logging for new backend services is disabled by default'; 'Cloud Armor log generation is subject to the log sampling rate configured for your load balancer ... If you reduce the sampling rate ... your Cloud Armor requests logs are sampled at that reduced rate'

probe: Cloud Armor L7 WAF / Adaptive Protection require an attached security policy on an external Application Load Balancer, while the default *.run.app Cloud Run URL bypasses the LB entirely — so the default Cloud Run deployment has no WAF, no L7 DDoS, and no cost-abuse protection in front of it
finding: All three sub-claims are confirmed by Google's own docs. (1) Cloud Armor security policies — including OWASP preconfigured WAF rules (evaluatePreconfiguredWaf), rate-limiting, and Adaptive Protection L7 ML — only take effect when attached to the BACKEND SERVICE of an external Application Load Balancer; the overview states 'You can attach security policies to the backend services of [these] load balancers.' For serverless, this means a Serverless NEG behind a global/classic external Application Load Balancer. None of this exists for a bare Cloud Run service. (2) Cloud Run's default ingress is 'all' — 'The default ingress paths and ingress setting allow any resource on the internet to reach your Cloud Run service' — and the default-on *.run.app URL is itself a public ingress path that does NOT traverse the LB. (3) The Cloud Armor integration doc states plainly: 'Users who have the default URL for a serverless application can bypass the load balancer and go directly to the service URL. This bypasses Cloud Armor security policies.' Closing the gap requires TWO non-default actions that must both be taken: deploy a Serverless-NEG + external ALB + attached Cloud Armor policy, AND set Cloud Run ingress to internal-and-cloud-load-balancing (a.k.a. internal-and-gclb) or disable the default URL (--no-default-url / run.googleapis.com/default-url-disabled: true). A default `gcloud run deploy --allow-unauthenticated` produces exactly the unprotected, LB-bypassing, publicly invokable endpoint. Adaptive Protection's L7 ML defense is itself separately opt-in per policy (--enable-layer7-ddos-defense), so even an LB-fronted service has no ML-based L7 DDoS detection until explicitly enabled.
gap: The secure topology is the assembly of several opt-in pieces, and the default of each piece points the wrong way: ingress defaults to 'all', the run.app URL is on by default and bypasses any LB, no Cloud Armor policy exists by default, and even when an ALB+policy is built the WAF preconfigured rules, rate-limiting, and Adaptive Protection L7 ML are each individually off until written. For a multi-team org under one Organization, nothing at the org/folder level forces this assembly: there is no default-on guardrail that prevents a team from shipping a naked run.app endpoint. (An Organization Policy constraint, run.allowedIngress, exists to restrict ingress, but it is opt-in and not applied by default.) The footgun is precisely the INTERACTION: each control is documented honestly in isolation, but the composite default is a public, WAF-less, rate-limit-less inference endpoint.
risk: For an AI-inference org, the default run.app endpoint is directly reachable from the internet with no L7 WAF (prompt-injection/path-traversal/OWASP filtering absent), no rate limiting, and no Adaptive Protection ML — exposing GPU-backed inference to credential-stuffing, scraping of proprietary model behavior, and especially cost-amplification / economic-denial-of-wallet attacks where each request burns expensive accelerator time. Even teams that build the 'correct' Cloud Armor + ALB stack remain bypassable until they ALSO flip ingress to internal-and-cloud-load-balancing or disable the default URL — a step easy to forget, leaving the protected LB path and an unprotected shadow path live simultaneously.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/armor/docs/integrating-cloud-armor — 'Users who have the default URL for a serverless application can bypass the load balancer and go directly to the service URL. This bypasses Cloud Armor security policies.' Also https://docs.cloud.google.com/run/docs/securing/ingress — 'The default ingress paths and ingress setting allow any resource on the internet to reach your Cloud Run service' and documents `internal-and-cloud-load-balancing` plus disabling the default `run.app` URL; and https://docs.cloud.google.com/armor/docs/cloud-armor-overview — security policies attach to LB backend services and L7 protection 'requires a user-configured security policy.'

probe: Always-on L3/L4 volumetric DDoS protection is real and default-on for load-balancer-fronted services, but is tied to the load balancer (and the richer always-on guarantees to Cloud Armor Enterprise) — it does not cover a bare run.app endpoint and is not L7
finding: Google provides genuine always-on, default-on protection from L3/L4 volumetric and network-protocol DDoS attacks for traffic that passes through global external Application Load Balancers, classic ALBs, and external proxy Network Load Balancers, 'regardless of tier.' This is a real strength and is automatic for LB-fronted workloads. However, it is (a) L3/L4 only — not application-layer; HTTP floods and L7 abuse are explicitly out of scope and require user-configured policies/Adaptive Protection; and (b) predicated on traffic actually reaching the LB. A default Cloud Run service accessed via its run.app URL does not sit behind one of these load balancers, so it does not benefit from this always-on edge absorption. Cloud Armor Enterprise extends always-on protection with telemetry, attack visibility, and Adaptive Protection, but the baseline L3/L4 absorption for ALBs is included without a subscription.
gap: The always-on guarantee is anchored to the load balancer, not to the workload. Because the default Cloud Run topology bypasses the LB, the strongest, default-on piece of GCP's edge protection does not apply to the most common (default) Cloud Run exposure. Teams may assume 'GCP has always-on DDoS protection' and not realize it only protects the LB path they never put their service behind.
risk: Operators conflate 'GCP absorbs DDoS by default' with 'my Cloud Run endpoint is protected,' but the always-on shield only covers traffic through the ALB. A run.app endpoint receives no edge volumetric absorption attributable to Cloud Armor and no L7 defense at all by default, leaving accelerator-backed inference exposed to both L7 floods and the cost-abuse vector — the very class of attack the default leaves unguarded.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/armor/docs/cloud-armor-overview — 'Cloud Armor provides always-on protection from L3 and L4 volumetric and network protocol-based DDoS attacks' for services behind load balancers; L7 protection 'requires a user-configured security policy.' See also https://docs.cloud.google.com/armor/docs/adaptive-protection-overview — Adaptive Protection (L7 ML) 'is enabled or disabled on a per-security-policy basis' (opt-in).

probe: First-party source control: Cloud Source Repositories (CSR) availability for a fresh org
finding: CSR is closed to new customers. Per the release notes: "Effective June 17, 2024, Cloud Source Repositories isn't available to new customers. If your organization hasn't previously used Cloud Source Repositories, you can't enable the API or use Cloud Source Repositories." Existing pre-June-2024 users may continue. A multi-team org spun up after that date therefore has NO general-purpose first-party Git hosting via CSR and must use GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket or pay for Secure Source Manager.
gap: For a fresh org holding proprietary source IP, the default and historically-recommended first-party Git host is gone. The supply-chain narrative now begins with a third-party VCS (GitHub/GitLab) and a connection/credential bridge, expanding the trust boundary beyond Google Cloud. There is no free, lightweight, per-project first-party repo equivalent.
risk: Source code IP lives outside GCP's IAM/VPC-SC perimeter by default; org must independently secure GitHub/GitLab org settings, branch protection, and access reviews on a platform GCP audit logs do not cover.
evidence: https://cloud.google.com/source-repositories/docs/release-notes

probe: First-party source control replacement: Secure Source Manager (SSM) cost/ergonomics as the CSR successor
finding: Google's recommended first-party replacement is Secure Source Manager, "a regionally deployed, single-tenant, managed source code repository" with Git, pull requests, issue tracking, IAM, VPC-SC and CMEK integration. But it is an instance-based, single-tenant service billed at a flat $1000/month per instance regardless of users, deployed to a specific region. It is not the lightweight per-project repo CSR was.
gap: The only first-party Git host for a fresh org carries a $1000/month/instance floor and an instance-provisioning model rather than CSR's free per-project repos. For a multi-team org, this pushes most teams toward external GitHub/GitLab and reserves SSM for IP that genuinely needs the in-perimeter, single-tenant, CMEK story — a deliberate cost/security tradeoff that is easy to mis-size.
risk: Cost pressure nudges teams to keep proprietary code on external SaaS VCS outside the GCP perimeter, weakening the VPC-SC / Access Transparency / CMEK protections that are the whole reason to stay first-party.
evidence: https://cloud.google.com/products/secure-source-manager/pricing

probe: CI/CD connection model: Cloud Build / Developer Connect GitHub App permission breadth and repo-level scoping
finding: Modern Cloud Build uses 2nd-gen repository connections via Developer Connect, which installs a GitHub App. The model supports granular scoping: during install you "select one or more repositories you want to grant access to" OR choose "All repositories." The GitHub-issued token is stored as a Secret Manager secret in your project. Admin setup needs roles/developerconnect.admin plus roles/secretmanager.admin (and projectIamAdmin for console auto-grants).
gap: Repo-level scoping exists but is opt-in at install time — the GitHub App UI offers an "All repositories" radio button that, if chosen, grants the connection (and anyone with access to the project's triggers/secrets) reach into every repo in the GitHub org. The docs do not foreground least-privilege repo selection as the default choice, so the over-broad option is one click away.
risk: An over-scoped connection lets a compromised or misconfigured Cloud Build project read source from every repo in the GitHub org; the stored Secret Manager token is a high-value credential bridging two clouds.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/developer-connect/docs/connect-github-repo

probe: Keyless integration: Workload Identity Federation for GitHub Actions — the attribute-condition footgun
finding: GCP documents the recommended keyless pattern (GitHub OIDC -> WIF pool -> SA impersonation, no long-lived JSON key) and explicitly warns about the shared-issuer footgun: "GitHub, GitLab SaaS, and HCP Terraform use a single issuer URL across all organizations... you must use an attribute condition that restricts access to tokens issued by your GitHub organization," recommending assertion.repository_owner=='ORGANIZATION'. It further warns that "using 'name' fields like repository and repository_owner increases the chances of cybersquatting and typosquatting attacks" and recommends numeric *_id fields. principalSet bindings on attribute.repository scope impersonation to a single repo.
gap: The control is correct and well-documented but is entirely opt-in and easy to get fatally wrong: a WIF provider created without an attribute condition (or with one keyed on the spoofable name fields) lets ANY GitHub repo on the public github.com issuer mint tokens that impersonate your SA. gcloud will create a provider without forcing a sufficiently restrictive condition. This is a Discoverable footgun mitigated only by reading the security note.
risk: A mis-scoped WIF provider is a cross-tenant impersonation hole: an attacker's public GitHub repo could obtain GCP SA credentials and reach proprietary model weights / source if the SA is privileged.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workload-identity-federation-with-deployment-pipelines

probe: Blast radius of the Cloud Build service account (historically over-privileged default)
finding: Historically Cloud Build ran builds as the Cloud Build legacy SA with broad permissions (e.g., access to any Cloud Storage bucket in the project). Over May–June 2024 Google changed the default to the Compute Engine default SA, and for organizations created on or after May 3, 2024 that SA is created WITHOUT the roles/editor grant. Opt-out/override is governed by org policy constraints constraints/cloudbuild.useComputeServiceAccount, constraints/cloudbuild.useBuildServiceAccount, and constraints/cloudbuild.disableCreateDefaultServiceAccount.
gap: The least-privilege default only applies to brand-new orgs/projects post-change. Projects without an organization, and projects that ran builds before the cutover, KEEP the legacy broad SA by default. Even the new Compute Engine default SA is a shared, project-wide identity used by builds, GCE, and other services — not a per-trigger least-privilege SA. Writing true least privilege still requires manually attaching a user-specified SA per trigger; the default remains a shared, often-Editor-laden identity in any pre-2024 footprint.
risk: A single poisoned build step runs as a project-wide SA; in legacy/no-org projects that SA often still holds Editor, so a malicious dependency or PR-triggered build can pivot to nearly all project resources including storage holding proprietary artifacts.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/build/docs/cloud-build-service-account-updates

probe: Build isolation: Cloud Build default (shared) pool vs private pools in your VPC
finding: By default builds run in a Google-hosted environment with public-internet egress, each build on its own isolated worker. Private pools are dedicated workers reachable from your VPC via VPC Network Peering, can drop public egress (egressOption: NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS), and can run inside a VPC Service Controls perimeter. The org policy constraint constraints/cloudbuild.allowedWorkerPools can force builds off the default pool onto approved private pools.
gap: Network isolation (private pools, no-egress, VPC-SC) is entirely opt-in and requires private services access / peering setup plus an org-policy enforcement to actually mandate it. The default pool has open internet egress, so unless an org explicitly enforces allowedWorkerPools, builds touching proprietary code/weights can exfiltrate to the public internet from a shared, externally-egressing worker.
risk: Default-pool builds with public egress are a data-exfiltration path for source/weights pulled into the build; without an org-policy mandate, individual teams silently default to the unisolated pool.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/build/docs/private-pools/private-pools-overview

probe: Build-secret exposure: substitutions vs Secret Manager integration vs log leakage
finding: Cloud Build integrates with Secret Manager via the availableSecrets field (secrets surfaced as secretEnv to specific steps, or mounted as volumes), and docs recommend it over trigger substitution variables. Docs explicitly warn that secret values echoed/logged are visible in build history and recommend pinning secret versions and scoping secrets to only the steps that need them.
gap: The secure path is opt-in and the insecure path (passing secrets as plaintext substitution variables / build args) is equally available and arguably easier for newcomers; there is no platform-level block on echoing a secret into the logs. Build logs are a real leakage surface, and substitution-based secrets are not redacted the way secretEnv values are.
risk: A careless build step can print a deploy token or API key into Cloud Build logs visible to anyone with log/build-history read, leaking credentials that reach proprietary systems.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/build/docs/securing-builds/use-secrets

probe: Build provenance: SLSA provenance generation and Binary Authorization image signing
finding: Cloud Build can generate build provenance meeting SLSA level 3 assurance (SLSA v0.1/v1.0) and can sign images / create attestations consumed by Binary Authorization for deploy-time enforcement. This is a genuine supply-chain strength. However provenance is OPT-IN: you must set options.requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED, it is generated ONLY for artifacts stored in Artifact Registry, and ONLY when images are declared in the build's images field (an explicit docker push step gets NO provenance).
gap: The strong SLSA-3 provenance and image-signing chain is off by default and silently does not apply to the common docker-push pattern or to non-Artifact-Registry destinations. Teams can believe they have provenance while their actual push path produces none; enforcement (Binary Authorization) is a separate opt-in policy that is not on by default.
risk: Without explicit configuration, deployed images carry no verifiable provenance; an unsigned or tampered artifact can reach production unless Binary Authorization is separately enabled and enforced.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/build/docs/securing-builds/generate-validate-build-provenance

probe: BYO prebuilt artifacts to Artifact Registry without granting Google source access
finding: An org can build images in its own CI (or locally), tag them for Artifact Registry, authenticate via gcloud credential helper or WIF, and docker push directly — needing only roles/artifactregistry.writer on the repo. No Cloud Build connection, no Developer Connect GitHub App, and no Google access to source are required. This cleanly lets a security-conscious org keep source on its own VCS and ship only built artifacts into GCP.
gap: The escape hatch works, but it forfeits the first-party supply-chain controls: images pushed via raw docker push get NO Cloud Build SLSA provenance (provenance only attaches via the Cloud Build images field), so the org must build its own attestation/SLSA tooling externally to feed Binary Authorization. The convenient path and the provenance-bearing path diverge.
risk: Choosing the BYO path (good for source confidentiality) means losing GCP-native provenance unless the org reconstructs SLSA attestation generation itself, leaving a gap that is easy to overlook.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/artifact-registry/docs/docker/pushing-and-pulling

probe: Auditing repo/supply-chain access via the connection (Cloud Audit Logs coverage)
finding: Both Cloud Build (cloudbuild.googleapis.com) and Developer Connect (developerconnect.googleapis.com) emit Cloud Audit Logs. Admin Activity logs (ADMIN_WRITE — e.g., creating connections/triggers) are on by default and cannot be disabled. But Data Access logs (DATA_READ/DATA_WRITE/ADMIN_READ — e.g., reading secrets/tokens, accessing connection data) are DISABLED by default and must be explicitly enabled. Crucially, GCP audit logs cover only the Google-Cloud side of the connection — actual repo reads/clones and pushes happen on GitHub/GitLab and are visible only in that provider's own audit log.
gap: Default visibility into the supply-chain connection is partial: data-access on the connection token/secret is off until manually enabled (a known GCP footgun), and the source-side of the trust boundary (who cloned/pushed which repo) is entirely outside GCP audit logs. For a multi-team org, full supply-chain auditing requires both enabling GCP Data Access logs AND federating GitHub/GitLab audit streams — neither is automatic.
risk: An attacker reading the stored GitHub token or accessing connection data may leave no Data Access log unless that tier was pre-enabled; and source exfiltration via the connected VCS is invisible to GCP entirely, creating a blind spot over proprietary code access.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/developer-connect/docs/audit-logging

probe: Resource hierarchy (Organization -> Folder -> Project -> resource) as the isolation/policy substrate for a multi-team org
finding: GCP provides a four-level tree: Organization (root, one per company domain), optional Folders (group legal entities/departments/teams/environments), Projects (the fundamental resource container and primary isolation unit), and resources. IAM allow policies AND Org Policies set at any node are inherited by all descendants; the effective allow policy is the union of policies set at the resource plus those inherited from parents. Projects are genuinely lightweight relative to AWS accounts (created via API in seconds, no separate billing/identity boundary required), and folders allow delegating ownership of an entire subtree to a department head. This is a real structural strength: a multi-team org can model team-per-folder, env-per-folder (prod/nonprod), grant baseline roles once at a folder, and let them flow down without per-project re-grant.
gap: Inheritance is additive-only for allow policies: a grant at the org/folder level cannot be subtracted at a child by an allow policy — you need IAM Deny policies or Org Policies to claw back. There is no hard tenant boundary equivalent to a separate account; all projects under one Organization share one identity domain and one super-admin blast radius. A misplaced broad grant high in the tree silently propagates to every team's project. Folder depth is also capped (max 10 levels, with practical limits well below that), and projects-per-org and per-folder quotas exist that a large multi-team org can hit.
risk: For an org holding proprietary IP across many teams, an over-broad role accidentally granted at the Organization or a shared parent folder (e.g. roles/viewer or roles/editor to a broad group) grants read/modify access to every team's source code, model weights, and documents in one stroke, with no per-project opt-out short of an explicit Deny policy.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/cloud-platform-resource-hierarchy — 'Access control policies (such as IAM roles) and organization policies applied at this level are inherited by every resource in the organization'; folders 'can model different legal entities, departments, and teams within a company.' See also https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/resource-hierarchy-access-control

probe: Default IAM grants on project creation and on default service accounts (Compute Engine / App Engine default SA historically gets Editor)
finding: Two distinct default-grant footguns. (1) Project creation: the creator of a project is automatically granted roles/owner on the new project — a basic role with thousands of permissions. (2) Default service accounts: the Compute Engine default SA (PROJECT_NUMBER-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com) and App Engine default SA were historically auto-granted roles/editor on the project, meaning any VM/workload using the default SA had read/write to most services. Google now ships the boolean Org Policy constraint iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts to suppress this, and enforces it by default — but ONLY for organizations created after May 3, 2024. Older orgs still get Editor on default SAs unless they explicitly enforce the constraint.
gap: The secure default is retroactively unavailable: any organization created before the 2024 cutoff carries the legacy Editor-on-default-SA behavior unless an admin discovers and enforces the constraint. The fix is opt-in for the large installed base most exposed to it. There is no automatic remediation or alert for existing orgs, and the project-creator auto-owner grant remains for everyone (no default constraint suppresses it).
risk: In an older org, a GKE node, Cloud Run service, or Compute VM left on the default SA effectively runs as Editor — a single compromised GPU training workload can read/modify Cloud Storage buckets and other services across the project, exposing model weights and IP. The auto-owner-to-creator pattern means anyone who can create projects becomes Owner of them, expanding the privileged footprint as teams self-serve projects.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/compute/docs/access/service-accounts — 'Depending on your organization policy configuration, the default service account might automatically be granted the Editor role on your project... We strongly recommend that you disable the automatic role grant by enforcing the iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts... If you created your organization after May 3, 2024, this constraint is enforced by default.' Project-creator owner grant: https://docs.cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/cloud-platform-resource-hierarchy

probe: Org Policy Service: SCP-equivalent guardrails, default enforcement state, and the secure-by-default baseline
finding: The Org Policy Service is GCP's declarative guardrail layer (the SCP analogue, arguably richer): boolean and list managed constraints plus custom constraints written in CEL, attachable at org/folder/project and inherited down the tree. Coverage is broad — disable SA key creation, require OS Login, restrict public IP on VMs, restrict allowed locations/regions, domain-restricted sharing (iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains), skip default network creation, restrict external IPs, etc. Google enforces a secure-by-default bundle on NEW organizations created on or after May 3, 2024 (announced March 20, 2024): seven managed constraints — iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation, iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyUpload, iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts, iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains, storage.uniformBucketLevelAccess, essentialcontacts.managed.allowedContactDomains, and compute.managed.restrictProtocolForwardingCreationForTypes.
gap: For any organization created before May 3, 2024, ZERO org policies are enforced by default — the entire guardrail surface is opt-in, and the blog explicitly says 'Existing organization resources are not impacted by this change.' Even for new orgs the bundle is narrow (seven constraints) and omits high-value guardrails like restricting public IPs, requiring OS Login, restricting regions for residency, or constraining Cloud Run allow-unauthenticated. Custom constraints support only a subset of CEL and a limited set of resource types, so not every risky action can actually be guardrailed declaratively.
risk: A multi-team org on a pre-2024 Organization that never explicitly authored org policies has no guardrails: teams can create long-lived SA JSON keys, share resources with arbitrary external Google identities, and deploy resources in any region — directly enabling credential exfiltration and residency violations for proprietary IP. The org must know to retrofit the baseline; nothing forces it.
evidence: https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/introducing-stronger-default-org-policies-for-our-customers — lists the enforced constraints and states 'Existing organization resources are not impacted by this change.' Overview: https://docs.cloud.google.com/organization-policy/overview

probe: IAM Deny policies and Principal Access Boundary (PAB) policies as composable scoping primitives layered on the hierarchy
finding: Beyond allow policies, GCP offers two newer composable primitives. IAM Deny policies attach to org/folder/project, are inherited downward, and are evaluated BEFORE allow policies — letting you carve out permissions (e.g. deny setIamPolicy to everyone except a break-glass group) regardless of granted roles, with exception principals and CEL conditions. Principal Access Boundary (PAB) policies attach to principal sets (not resources) and cap the set of resources a principal is eligible to access at all, regardless of roles — a true ceiling. Together with Org Policy and VPC Service Controls these give defense-in-depth: allow + deny + eligibility-boundary + data-perimeter.
gap: These are powerful but entirely opt-in and operationally complex: PAB is relatively new, attaches to principal sets rather than the resource tree (so it does NOT inherit like allow/deny policies — a different mental model that admins must learn), and Deny policies use a separate permission-string namespace and CEL conditions that are easy to get wrong. There is no default deny anywhere; the entire least-privilege ceiling must be authored. Reasoning about the combined evaluation of allow + deny + PAB + Org Policy + VPC-SC across an inherited hierarchy is genuinely hard and a common source of both over- and under-grants.
risk: The primitives exist to write least privilege, but the org bears full authoring burden and the multi-layer evaluation model makes it easy to believe a Deny or PAB is protecting IP when a scoping mistake (wrong principal set, condition that fails open) leaves it ineffective.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/deny-overview — 'IAM always checks relevant deny policies before checking relevant allow policies.' PAB: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/principal-access-boundary-policies. Pattern guidance: https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/just-say-no-build-defense-in-depth-with-iam-deny-and-org-policies

probe: Custom roles: can least privilege actually be WRITTEN, and at what cost?
finding: GCP supports custom IAM roles at the organization and project level, assembled from individual permissions, alongside ~hundreds of granular predefined roles. Google explicitly warns against the three basic roles (Owner/Editor/Viewer): 'Basic roles include thousands of permissions across all Google Cloud services. In production environments, do not grant basic roles unless there is no alternative.' So least privilege is genuinely expressible via predefined + custom roles.
gap: Custom roles carry real ergonomic friction: (1) they CANNOT be defined at the folder level — only org or project — so a per-team-folder least-privilege role cannot be authored where the team boundary lives; you duplicate it per project or define it org-wide. (2) Not all permissions support custom roles (NOT_SUPPORTED / TESTING tiers), so some actions are only grantable via broad predefined roles. (3) Maintenance burden falls on the customer: as services add permissions, custom roles drift and must be manually reconciled, which in practice pushes teams back toward the over-broad basic roles Google warns against.
risk: Because the genuinely correct boundary (the team folder) can't hold a custom role, and maintenance is manual, teams under time pressure default to roles/editor or roles/owner — re-introducing the thousands-of-permissions blast radius over proprietary code and model weights that custom roles were meant to avoid.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/roles-overview — quotes above on basic roles; custom roles 'can be created at organization or project level'; permissions carry support levels SUPPORTED/TESTING/NOT_SUPPORTED and ALPHA/BETA permissions should not be used in production.

probe: Central identity: Cloud Identity / Google Workspace as IdP, federation/SSO to external IdP, SCIM provisioning, groups for IAM
finding: Google Cloud's identity plane is Cloud Identity / Google Workspace. It supports SSO to external IdPs (Okta, Microsoft Entra ID, AD FS) via SAML 2.0 and OIDC, including multiple IdPs concurrently and per-OU/per-group SSO profiles. Groups synced from the IdP can be referenced directly in IAM bindings (google.groups), so access is managed by group membership rather than per-user grants. Two federation models exist: directory provisioning into Cloud Identity (with auto-provisioning to avoid casing/spelling drift), and Workforce Identity Federation, which lets external IdP identities access GCP without syncing them into Cloud Identity at all.
gap: SCIM-style auto-provisioning and SSO are configured by the customer and are not on by default; a fresh Cloud Identity tenant uses Google as the IdP with local accounts unless federation is set up. Mixing directory-synced identities and Workforce Identity Federation creates two parallel identity surfaces to audit. The genuine concentration risk is that all projects in the org trust one identity domain — there is no per-project identity boundary, so the IdP and its admins are a single point of compromise for the whole org's IP.
risk: Misconfigured federation (e.g. an external IdP group mapped to a broad GCP group, or auto-provisioning creating unintended principals) can grant access to many teams' resources at once. A compromise of the upstream IdP (Okta/Entra) or its trust configuration is a path to the entire GCP org.
evidence: https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/google-now-supports-multi-idp-sso-in-google-workspace-and-google-cloud ; Workforce Identity Federation: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/configuring-workforce-identity-federation ; federation best practices: https://cloud.google.com/architecture/identity/best-practices-for-federating

probe: Super-admin blast radius (Cloud Identity / Workspace super administrator) vs Organization Administrator IAM role
finding: The Cloud Identity / Workspace super administrator is the apex identity: it controls the identity domain, user lifecycle, and SSO/MFA settings, and can recover or claim the Organization Administrator IAM role — making it effectively able to bootstrap full control of the GCP Organization. Google deliberately separates it from the day-to-day Organization Administrator IAM role and documents strong controls: hardware-security-key MFA, dedicated non-personal admin accounts, short sign-in sessions, a small number of accounts, and a break-glass recovery process. Google's guidance is to put Organization Administrators in a private admin group but NOT the super admin.
gap: All controls (MFA enforcement, account count, session length, separation from Org Admin) are operational best-practices the customer must implement; none are enforced by default at tenant creation, and a brand-new Cloud Identity tenant's initial super admin is the person who signed up — often a personal-style account without a hardware key. The super admin sits outside the GCP IAM/Org Policy guardrail model (org policies do not constrain Workspace-level super-admin actions), so it is a guardrail-bypassing single point of failure for the whole multi-team org.
risk: Compromise or misuse of one super admin account yields control over the org's entire identity plane and a path to all projects' IP, plus the ability to alter SSO/MFA and tamper with the audit configuration — the maximal blast radius in the substrate, and it is not contained by the same policies that govern everything below it.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/super-admin-best-practices — 'Enforce multi-factor authentication on your super admin accounts' using 'a security key or other physical authentication device'; create a private Google Cloud administrator group and add Organization Administrators 'but not your super admin user.'

probe: Policy Intelligence (IAM Recommender, Policy Analyzer, Policy Troubleshooter) for least privilege at scale
finding: Policy Intelligence is a genuine differentiator for operating least privilege across a large hierarchy. IAM Recommender uses ML over the last 90 days of actual permission usage to flag over-provisioned roles and propose tighter replacements (and can be bulk-applied). Policy Analyzer answers 'who can access this resource and what can they do,' expanding groups and roles across the inherited hierarchy. Policy Troubleshooter explains why a specific request was allowed or denied, accounting for allow, deny, and PAB policies. Together they make the otherwise-hard task of right-sizing IAM across many projects tractable.
gap: These are detective/advisory, not preventive — they recommend, they do not enforce, so an org can ignore every recommendation and remain over-permissioned. Recommendations require ~90 days of usage data, so newly granted or rarely exercised roles (e.g. break-glass, quarterly jobs) are under-observed and may be wrongly flagged as removable or never surfaced. Full Policy Analyzer / recommendation capabilities and history sit behind Security Command Center Premium/Enterprise tiers, so the strongest at-scale analysis is gated behind paid SCC for many orgs.
risk: Without acting on recommendations, the org accumulates standing over-privilege over its IP; relying on usage-based pruning can either leave dormant-but-legitimate access flagged (operational risk) or fail to catch privilege that simply hasn't been exercised by an attacker yet.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/policy-intelligence/docs/overview ; role recommendations: https://docs.cloud.google.com/policy-intelligence/docs/role-recommendations-overview ; troubleshooting access: https://docs.cloud.google.com/policy-intelligence/docs/troubleshoot-access

probe: Assured Workloads as a compliance-substrate overlay (residency + personnel constraints enforced via the hierarchy)
finding: Assured Workloads overlays compliance control packages onto a folder, so all projects/resources inside inherit the package's guardrails. Packages span regional data boundaries (data residency with optional personnel controls), regulatory data boundaries (e.g. FedRAMP Moderate/High, IL4/IL5, EU Data Boundary), and Sovereign Controls by Partners (adding Cloud EKM, Cloud HSM, Key Access Justifications). Controls are enforced using Org Policy constraints plus platform features, and personnel constraints (e.g. US-persons-only support access for IL4) are applied at Google's infrastructure level without customer configuration.
gap: Assured Workloads is opt-in, applies per-folder (not org-wide by default), and is oriented to formal compliance regimes (FedRAMP/IL/EU sovereignty) rather than a generic 'protect our proprietary IP' posture — a commercial multi-team org gets no benefit unless it deliberately adopts a package, and doing so constrains available services/regions, which can break general AI/GPU and serverless workloads. It is also a higher-cost, more restrictive overlay, so it is unlikely to be the substrate-wide default for an IP-protection use case that isn't bound by a specific regulation.
risk: For the stated context (proprietary IP, not a regulated workload), Assured Workloads is unlikely to be in play, so the residency/personnel guarantees it provides are not protecting the IP unless the org specifically opts a folder into a package and accepts the service/region restrictions.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/assured-workloads/docs/overview ; control packages: https://docs.cloud.google.com/assured-workloads/docs/control-packages — 'When you create an Assured Workloads folder for a specific control package, the controls within the control package define guardrails for all projects and resources within the folder.'

probe: VPC Service Controls as the data-perimeter layer of the substrate (cross-reference: network isolation)
finding: VPC Service Controls (VPC-SC) is GCP's data-exfiltration perimeter and a real substrate-level strength: it draws an API-level perimeter around a set of projects so that managed services (Cloud Storage, BigQuery, Vertex AI, etc.) can only be reached from inside the perimeter, independent of IAM. It blocks credential-based exfiltration even with valid IAM permissions, supports ingress/egress rules and access levels, and offers a dry-run mode to observe violations before enforcing. For protecting model weights and source in Cloud Storage, Google positions VPC-SC as its strongest exfiltration control because it operates at the API layer regardless of IAM.
gap: VPC-SC is entirely opt-in and notoriously hard to operate at scale: nothing is perimetered by default, every protected service and project must be enumerated, and ingress/egress rules plus access levels are intricate — misconfiguration commonly either breaks legitimate access or, worse, leaves a believed-protected service outside the perimeter. New services and project moves can silently fall outside an existing perimeter. It governs only supported Google-managed services (coverage varies by service), so it is not a universal data boundary.
risk: Without a correctly scoped enforced perimeter, stolen credentials or a malicious insider with read on a bucket can pull proprietary model weights/source out to the public internet — precisely the threat VPC-SC exists to stop. The opt-in, error-prone nature means many orgs either lack it or run a perimeter with gaps they believe are closed.
evidence: https://docs.cloud.google.com/vpc-service-controls/docs/overview ; perimeter configuration / enforced vs dry-run: https://docs.cloud.google.com/vpc-service-controls/docs/service-perimeters
